### nicld — 2013-11-24T04:03:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-sleep.8857/post-101513>

When my thyroid was off, I was waking up about 2:30 am and had a hard time getting back to sleep. The past couple days it has been getting better but I want to figure out why to stop it from happening again. I am really liking sleep again.

From some of the webinars I have learned that growth hormone is released between 2-5am but only in stages 3-4 of sleep. This in turned lets leptin work which in turn tell cortisol levels to start rising telling one to wake up.

I do wear blue blocker glasses at night and have my last meal 3-4 hours before bed.

My cortisol levels are good and I am waiting for the salivary melatonin tests. I think my thyroid is better now and I am on Pg cream.

So, what is going on hormonally that wakes my up at this time and keeps me up? I am having a hard time figuring that out. Still have some more webinars to listen to and them listen too again.

If anybody has any insights that they can share, I would appreciate it.

### caroline — 2013-11-24T04:51:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-sleep.8857/post-101516>

Just a thought .... I do way better if I eat earlier - like around 4:30 or 5:00pm. Much after 5 and I notice a difference. It may not be possible to eat that early I realise.

# Thread: Mutagenesis might be worse than GMO food
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mutagenesis-might-be-worse-than-gmo-food.8865/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ATL_Paleo — 2013-11-25T03:05:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mutagenesis-might-be-worse-than-gmo-food.8865/post-101569>

[http://www.businessweek.com/article...t-crop-developers-in-global-seed-market#r=rss](http://www.businessweek.com/articles/2013-11-21/monsanto-vs-dot-mutant-crop-developers-in-global-seed-market#r=rss)

Last July, when Monsanto (MON) withdrew its applications to sell genetically modified biotech seeds in the European Union, the move opened the way for competitors to challenge Monsantoâ€™s market share.

As opposition to genetically modified crops has spread across Europe and the world, leading chemical companies including BASF (BASFY) and DuPont (DD) have turned to mutagenesisâ€”a technique that mimics the sunâ€™s irradiation of plantsâ€”to create herbicide-resistant crops. The process, which faces almost no regulation, creates opportunities for companies to grab a bigger share of the $34 billion global commercial seed market. But some scientists say mutant crops are more likely to pose health risks than genetically modified ones.

Mutagenesis isnâ€™t new: Breeders have relied on it for decades to produce thousands of varieties of lettuce, oats, rice, and other crops. BASF today licenses its technologies to 40 of the worldâ€™s biggest seed companies, including DuPont and Switzerlandâ€™s Syngenta (SYENF), which in turn sell high volumes of mutant breeds, ranging from wheat to sunflowers, in markets that reject genetically engineered seeds.

The EU has approved planting only one of Monsantoâ€™s genetically modified crop varieties in two decades, prompting its decision to withdraw eight pending GMO requests last summer. Itâ€™s not the only company confronting roadblocks with GMOs in Europe. BASF last year decided to move its plant science division, dedicated to engineered crops, to the U.S. from Germany. Even in the U.S., bills pending in 26 state legislatures and before Congress would, if passed, require labels on genetically modified foods. No such disclosures are required on ingredients derived from mutant crops.

All breeding techniques can create plants with increased levels of naturally occurring toxins or with proteins known to cause allergic reactions. Reports from the National Academy of Sciences, representing the consensus of experts in the field, say the risk of creating unintended health effects is greater from mutagenesis than any other technique, including genetic modification. Mutagenesis deletes and rearranges hundreds or thousands of genes randomly, spawning mutations that are less precise than GMOs. The academy has warned that regulating genetically modified crops while giving a pass to mutant products isnâ€™t scientifically justified.

The risks associated with mutagenesis, the academy also says, are small relative to the incidence of food-borne illnesses such as salmonella. BASF maintains the crops are safe for consumers and the environment. â€œThis has been a technique used for many decades without issue, without concern,â€￾ Bryant says.

In addition to the regulatory-free environment they operate in, mutant crops are also gaining in popularity because theyâ€™re cheaper to produce. Monsanto spends anywhere from $150 million to $200 million to launch a single genetically engineered product. Japan, by comparison, invested $69 million from 1959 to 2001 on mutant breeds that yielded $62 billion worth of products over that period, according to data from the United Nationsâ€™ Nuclear Techniques in Food and Agriculture program.

â€œThese difficulties in getting a GMO to the market, we donâ€™t have it in mutation breeding,â€￾ says Pierre Lagoda, who heads up the UN program. Thatâ€™s spurred even more interest in the mutant varieties, he says. In 2013 alone, Lagodaâ€™s program has received requests to help irradiate a record 31 plant species ranging from sugar beets from Poland to potatoes from Kenya.

â€œThe current regulations are a huge incentive to go back and do things the old way,â€￾ including mutagenesis, says Wayne Parrott, a professor of crop science at the University of Georgia in Athens. Monsanto has also used mutation breeding, says the companyâ€™s chief technology officer, Robb Fraley.

Industry experts say breeding and biotechnology are starting to converge. Over the past five years, breeders have increasingly used molecular markers and sequenced genomes of corn and other crops to improve crossbreeding, making conventional breeding more like genetic engineering. â€œThere is not a black line between biotechnology and nonbiotechnology,â€￾ says Paul Schickler, president of DuPontâ€™s Pioneer seed unit. â€œItâ€™s a continuum.â€￾


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### healthman — 2014-01-16T15:35:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-checkout-my-labs.8885/post-105902>

Thanks HoneyChild - are there any good points on my results?
My testosterone continues to dive not matter what protocol's I implement??

### HoneyChild — 2014-01-16T17:32:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-checkout-my-labs.8885/post-105916>

> [healthman said:](/goto/post?id=105902)
>
> Thanks HoneyChild - are there any good points on my results?

Sorry, I don't know enough about tests yet. Your potassium looks not too bad although I've heard that people do better at about 4.5.

Your MCV is low and that generally means iron deficiency.

"The most common causes of microcytic anemia are iron deficiency (due to inadequate dietary intake, gastrointestinal blood loss, or menstrual blood loss), thalassemia, sideroblastic anemia or chronic disease. In iron deficiency anemia (microcytic anemia), it can be as low as 60 to 70 femtolitres. In some cases of thalassemia, the MCV may be low even though the patient is not iron deficient."
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mean\_corpuscular\_volume](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mean_corpuscular_volume)

MCH is only just above the minimum so that probably too low, caused by iron deficiency.

"The mean corpuscular hemoglobin, or "mean cell hemoglobin" (MCH), is the average mass of hemoglobin per red blood cell in a sample of blood. It is reported as part of a standard complete blood count. MCH value is diminished in hypochromic anemias."
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mean\_corpuscular\_hemoglobin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mean_corpuscular_hemoglobin)

If you have an iron deficiency it could be because of some kind of infection, check out this link:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anemia\_of\_chronic\_disease](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anemia_of_chronic_disease)

# Thread: Link Between Red Cell Distribution Width Levels, Depression
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/link-between-red-cell-distribution-width-levels-depression.8891/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2013-11-26T18:29:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/link-between-red-cell-distribution-width-levels-depression.8891/post-101680>

Nov. 18, 2013 â€” Researchers at the Intermountain Medical Center Heart Institute have discovered a link between elevated red cell distribution width levels and depression in patients being treated for heart disease. This new discovery can help physicians provide earlier diagnosis and treatment for possible depression in heart patients.

Red cell distribution width or RDW is a parameter that measures variation in red blood cell size or red blood cell volume. A high RDW (over 14.5%) means that the red blood cells vary a lot in size. A normal RDW is 11.6 to 14.6%, but researchers from the Intermountain Medical Center Heart Institute found that patients with a RDW level greater than or equal to 12.9% had an increased risk for depression.

The new study found that the higher the RDW, the greater the risk for depression for patients. This is the first time this association has been discovered.

Results of the study will be presented during the American Heart Association Scientific Sessions in Dallas, on Monday, November 18 at 9:30 am, ET.

"Elevated red blood distribution widths are associated with anemia, but it also appears to be associated with other poor outcomes, like heart attacks, heart failure, death and now depression," said Heidi May, PhD, MSPH, the study's principal researcher at the Intermountain Medical Center Heart Institute in Murray, Utah.

This study looked at 43,226 patients and evaluated them for an average of 5.3 years, identifying RDW levels of patients at time of diagnosis and comparing them to a follow-up diagnosis of depression.

RDW is routinely tested as part of the complete blood count panel and is generally used in combination with other tests to differentiate forms of anemia. The tests detect pulses that are produced by red blood cells. The stronger the pulses are, the greater the red blood cells are in size. Likewise, the weaker the pulses are, the smaller the red blood cells are in size.

"Patients and physicians should be more aware that depression may be one of those poor outcomes and should be more diligent in screening for depression among patients who have an elevated RDW," Dr. May said.

Previous studies have also shown RDW to be a powerful indicator of poor outcomes, particularly among cardiovascular patients. However, depression has never been studied as a possible outcome for heart patients, according to Dr. May.

"With these findings, physicians should be more aware of this association and note that heart patients with an elevated RDW are at a higher risk for depression," she said. "This should encourage physicians to be more diligent in screening for depression and treating it accordingly."

"This study is important as it's the first to show an association between elevated RDW and depression in heart patients," said Dr. May. "Our hope is that other studies can be done to look at this association in different populations, even a more general medical population, to see if an association remains."

The findings of this study persisted despite adjustment for risk factors, medications, and indicators of other diseases. Additional studies will be required to determine if the association is causal, wherein depression is a result of abnormal red cell size, anemia or some other co-morbidity.

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2013/11/131118111750.htm](http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2013/11/131118111750.htm)

### nonchalant — 2013-11-27T01:23:57+0200

# Thread: Eating habits of mid-Victorians
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-habits-of-mid-victorians.8893/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2013-11-26T21:44:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-habits-of-mid-victorians.8893/post-101683>

Interesting research paper on how the mid-Victorians in U.K. worked, ate, lived and died:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2672390/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2672390/)

Check out their seafood eating habits:

"The herring was one of the most important fish in the Victorian urban diet; fresh in the autumn, winter and spring; dried and salted (red herring) or pickled/soused all year round. Red herrings were a staple of the working class diet throughout the year because they were easily cooked (e.g. Idylls of the Poor). Other favourites were cheap and easily obtainable varieties with better keeping qualities than the more vulnerable white fish, including sprats, eels, and shellfish (oysters, mussels, cockles, whelks). Of the white fish consumed, cod, haddock and John Dory were preferred. Typically, and unlike today, the whole fish was consumed including heads and roes \[22\]. Fish was available from Monday evening to Friday evening; with broken and day old fish or eels and shoreline shellfish available on Saturdays, as fishermen did not go out over the weekends \[45\]."

The research also includes a sketch of the health crash in late-Victorian era (with urban/environment development, cheap food production, sugar consumption). To quote: "The decline was astonishingly rapid."

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-26T21:48:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-habits-of-mid-victorians.8893/post-101684>

Think about the old Irish song Molly Mallone - not really surprised

"In Dublin's fair city,
Where the girls are so pretty,
I first set my eyes on sweet Molly Malone,
As she wheeled her wheel-barrow,
Through streets broad and narrow,
Crying, "Cockles and mussels, alive, alive, oh!"
"Alive, alive, oh,
Alive, alive, oh,"
Crying "Cockles and mussels, alive, alive, oh".
She was a fishmonger,
But sure 'twas no wonder,
For so were her father and mother before,
And they each wheeled their barrows,
Through streets broad and narrow,
Crying, "Cockles and mussels, alive, alive, oh!"
(chorus)
She died of a fever,
And none could relieve her,
And that was the end of sweet Molly Malone.
But her ghost wheels her barrow,
Through streets broad and narrow,
Crying, "Cockles and mussels, alive, alive, oh!"

### nonchalant — 2013-11-27T01:27:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-habits-of-mid-victorians.8893/post-101699>

### Huck — 2013-11-27T01:21:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-immune-system.8896/post-101697>

What labs would show the state of my immune system?

### nonchalant — 2013-11-27T01:51:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-immune-system.8896/post-101700>

### Grizz — 2013-12-07T21:38:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-immune-system.8896/post-102746>

nonchalant is correct.

Do your Vitamin D3 blood test to verify that your D3 levels are between 70 to 80 ng/ml. You can do the inexpensive home test:
[https://vitamindcouncil.zrtlab.com/](https://vitamindcouncil.zrtlab.com/)

I recommend starting with 10,000 IU D3 Geltabs daily for 3 months, then get your blood tested. Adjust your dosage up or down as required. Each 1,000 IU change in dosage will change our blood test reading by 10 ng/ml. Most people need between 8,000 IU to 12,000 IU daily. ( less in the summer if you are exposed directly to sunlight )

Complete details on Vitamin D3 here:
[http://tinyurl.com/Vitamin-D-Report](http://tinyurl.com/Vitamin-D-Report)

Dr. Kruse Iodine Report:
[http://www.jackkruse.com/the-sunshine-of-your-life/](http://www.jackkruse.com/the-sunshine-of-your-life/)

**My own experience with D3:**
Before taking Vitamin D3, I used to get SEVERE chest colds every winter & spring. So severe that I frequently lost my voice and had severe difficulty breathing. Now since starting D3 about 8 years ago, I have not suffered a single chest cold at all, and at worst, I just get the sniffles and a runny nose. I also don't take flu shots, and have not caught the flu in 8 years. I used to also catch the flu at least every 3 to 4 years. Not anymore.

I used to be so afraid of catching a chest cold that I sat in the back of Church to avoid people coughing. Not any more. Now I LAUGH at the cold & flu. My immunity is fantastic. I take 10,000 IU daily D3 Geltabs made with olive oil, from Amazon.Com. I order enough to last several years at a time.

**Read this excellent report:**

> Epidemic of Vitamin D deficiency sweeping the world
> by Mike Adams, the Health Ranger, NaturalNews Editor
>
> (NaturalNews) There is an epidemic of vitamin D deficiency sweeping across our modern world, and it's an epidemic of such depth and seriousness that it makes the H1N1 swine flu epidemic look like a case of the sniffles by comparison. Vitamin D deficiency is not only alarmingly widespread, it's also a root cause of many other serious diseases such as cancer, diabetes, osteoporosis and heart disease.
>
> A new study published in the March, 2010 issue of the Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism found that a jaw-dropping 59 percent of the population is vitamin D deficient. In addition, nearly 25 percent of the study subjects were found to have extremely low levels of vitamin D.
>
> Lead author of the study, Dr. Richard Kremer at the McGill University Health Center, said "Abnormal levels of vitamin D are associated with a whole spectrum of diseases, including cancer, osteoporosis, and diabetes, as well as cardiovascular and autoimmune disorders."
>
> This new study also documents a clear link between vitamin D deficiency and stored body fat. This supports a theory I've espoused here on NaturalNews for many years: That sunshine actually promote body fat loss. Vitamin D may be the hormonal mechanism by which this fat loss phenomenon operates.
>
> The research findings on vitamin D, by the way, get even better...
>
> Activator for the immune system
> Recent research carried out at the University of Copenhagen has revealed that vitamin D activates the immune system by "arming" T cells to fight off infections.
>
> This new research, led by Professor Carsten Geisler from the Department of International Health, Immunology and Microbiology at the University of Copenhagen, found that without vitamin D, the immune system's T cells remain dormant, offering little or no protection against invading microorganisms and viruses. But with vitamin D in the bloodstream, T cells become "armed" and begin seeking out invaders that are then destroyed and carried out of the body.
> Rest of the report:
> [http://www.naturalnews.com/z028357\_vitamin\_D\_deficiency.html](http://www.naturalnews.com/z028357_vitamin_D_deficiency.html)

Grizz

### Huck — 2013-12-08T13:22:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-immune-system.8896/post-102832>

Let me explain my motivation for the original question.

For treating/preventing cancer, there is a lot of information on supplements and foods that are helpful, but how do we test to see what is working, if anything?

So I switched it around and looked at what causes/allows cancer to grow.

-   Weakened immune system
-   Blood glucose
-   Blood supply

Of those 3, blood glucose is the easiest to test. Prick your finger, put a drop of blood on a test strip and stick it in the meter. But I don't know the best and least expensive labs for testing the immune system and blood supply.

It seems to me that if I can show my immune system is getting stronger, then I'm probably on the right track with diet and supplements. So I need to know what labs will show me this. From searching the web, I found references that suggested the following labs:

-   Complete Blood Count (CBC)
-   Immunoglobulin Tests
-   Complement Assay
-   Free light chain serum test ([http://www.mayoclinic.org/news2012-rst/6908.html](http://www.mayoclinic.org/news2012-rst/6908.html))

I think labs to measure t cells and nk cells would also be good.

What I was hoping was that with the wide experience of the people here, we could put together a list of labs that would show if our diet is working. There are lots of books and web site that provide lists of foods and supplements that cancer patients should take, but little information on how to test to see if they are helping. My guess is that just as drugs often lose their effect over time, foods/supplements do the same. So maybe to be effective, different supplements or combinations of supplements are most beneficial. Without labs, is the only option to see if cancer gets worse?

With blood supply.

> Cancer researchers studying the conditions necessary for cancer metastasis have discovered that one of the critical events required is the growth of a new network of blood vessels. This process of forming new blood vessels is called angiogenesis. [http://www.cancer.gov/cancertopics/understandingcancer/angiogenesis/page2](http://www.cancer.gov/cancertopics/understandingcancer/angiogenesis/page2)

Certain foods and drugs are anti-angiogenesis. So I will also try to put together a list of labs that can help determine if they (foods and supplements) are successfully preventing angiogenesis.

Thanks

When asked who one's heroes are, many bring up sports or movie personalities and hold them up as examples of what one should aspire to, and be inspired by.

Fair enough.

I suppose, in a superfluous sort of way, I can get a rise out of a good performance and try and apply it to my life. I mean, watching David Gilmore of Pink Floyd shred a guitar solo can actually bring a tear to my eye and get me to walk in the other room, pick up my Strat andâ€¦ well, I may never play like him, but you get the picture.

In my mind's eye, a scientist/researcher/inventor, indeed - _innovator_, tops my list of heroes. Their work and discoveries can affect billions of people, and most of the time they are largely unsung.

# Thread: Don't Worry--GMOs are Good for You!

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

### Gracelind — 2013-11-28T22:40:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-worry-gmos-are-good-for-you.8921/post-101833>

GMOs have no detrimental health effects and are simply the target of "powerful" and "motivated" groups "trying to scare home cooks" such as . . . the dastardly Dr Bronner's Magic Soap!

As J Justin Wilson of the Center for Consumer Freedom writes in [One thing cooks shouldn’t worry about this Thanksgiving](http://dailycaller.com/2013/11/27/one-thing-cooks-shouldnt-worry-about-this-thanksgiving/):

_Campaigns to label GMOs are, as one Seattle Times reporter noted, little more than efforts by organic food interests to place labels on their competition. It’s the worst kind of government cronyism dressed up in flowery rhetoric about a “right to know” that hides the scaremongering and organic industry profiteering._

As always, consider the source: the Center for Consumer Freedom was founded with a grant from Philip Morris and represents restaurant, meat and alcoholic beverage industries. [http://www.sourcewatch.org/index.php?title=Center\_for\_Consumer\_Freedom](http://www.sourcewatch.org/index.php?title=Center_for_Consumer_Freedom)

Somehow this column still fails to persuade me to drop the "flowery" notion that we all have the right to know where our food came from, how it was produced, and what it contains.

Hi Guys!

I recently had some blood tests done as well as a cortisol saliva test. The results showed that my cortisol levels were high from 8am-8pm (12 hour test). I have no problem at all falling asleep, I wake up a few times during the night but roll over and fall back to sleep easily, in the morning I wake up feeling totally unrefreshed.... takes about 30-60min to feel ok.

My doc thinks I have adrenal fatigue issues.

I've read Jacks article on HPA axis problems being the cause of AF. So I want to start the Leptin Rx. I'm 6'2", tall, lanky and skinny (72kg). When it comes to the 50g carb intake for the Leptin Rx Jack says it is "not strict for the underweight".

So how high can I go for my carb intake while getting good results? I usually eat around 150g carbs a day.

Any other advince, info, insights, etc on reducing high cortisol and healing adrenals is appreciated!

Thanks

Oz

### Andis — 2013-12-03T15:57:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/walking.8932/post-102177>

IMO walking isn't an exercise. Problems may occur if it's treated as an exercise, with unnecessary goals and "more is better" attitude. It's good if makes you spend more time in nature and might be bad if puts you on a treadmill.

### timmothysmith01 — 2023-01-10T16:31:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/walking.8932/post-318811>

[WALKING LUNGES: SINGLE-LEG WORKOUT](https://www.borntough.com/blogs/news/kill-your-leg-workout-with-these-15-best-leg-exercises)

-   Standing straight, space your shoulders widely. Keep your hands by your sides at all times.
-   Step forward and put weight on your heels while using your right leg.
-   Bend your right leg to lower your body, then pause for a moment.
-   Pressing the left foot into the ground, keep it forward as you stand.
-   Alternately move your other leg in the same manner.

### healthman — 2013-12-01T11:09:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep.8933/post-101942>

All my life I have had terrible sleep
But after I started epi paleo and my evening supplements
My sleep improved ten fold until the last week when it has
turned into a nightmare again.
I have been falling to sleep between 8 & 9 o'clock but wake up
between 12 & 1 am & doesn't what I do I cannot go back to sleep
I am taking magnesium & vitamin c before bed plus some raw honey
Any suggestions to help would be greatly appreciated.

### caroline — 2013-12-01T11:15:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep.8933/post-101944>

### healthman — 2013-12-01T12:09:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep.8933/post-101951>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=101944)
>
> What time is your last meal? Maybe taking supplements earlier?

Hi Caroline
Last meal is around 6ish.
Im surprised as this seemed to be working for me for a while, its just this last week than has gone right out the window?

Thanks

### caroline — 2013-12-01T12:18:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep.8933/post-101952>

What is your non native EMF situation? smart meter? neighbours smart meter? something new at work? transition metals?
something has changed in your environment....... you need to be a detective!

iphone, ipad, TV where you sleep? even your watch? Something has changed......

Can you eat earlier? Have you changed what you are eating? I eat about 4:30 - that works really much better for me.

Are you eating a large meal in the evening? maybe too heavy?

What about emotional stuff in your life? you are male - right?

### Huck — 2013-12-01T16:12:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep.8933/post-101956>

Could just be a normal stage. When I first started, I needed a handful of different supplements to get to sleep. After a year, I stopped a couple of the supplements and it took my body a while to adjust. Then about 2 months ago I stopped all sleep supplements and again it took a while to adjust. Now I fall asleep easily with no supplements.

I will go for months where I sleep straight through the night. Then for no obvious (to me) reason, I start waking up at 4 am every morning. Or maybe sleep for a couple of hours and wake up. As your brain heals, your sleep should get better and better. Sleep goes through specific cycles and maybe you are having trouble transitioning from one phase to another. There are also specific hormonal changes that take place during the night and I think that surge of hormones is what wakes some people up.

One thing you might try is meditation or self hypnosis. When you wake up and can't get back to sleep, use one of these tools to relax your body and mind and see if that will put you back to sleep. Self hypnosis is pretty easy. Just start with your toes and imagine them feeling warm and relaxed. Then slowly move up your body feeling each part getting warmer and more relaxed. Feel your body getting heavier and heavier as you sink into the bed. Quite often I would fall asleep before I got all the way to my head.

If you get all the way through your body and are feeling warm and relaxed, then picture yourself lying on your bed in an elevator on the 10th floor. Start the elevator going down slowly, keeping your eyes closed. As it goes down, feel your body getting heavier, warmer, and more relaxed. Drive all thoughts from your brain except the feeling of moving slowly downward and getting heavier and more relaxed. Hopefully, you will fall asleep before you get to the 1st floor.

### SeaHorse — 2013-12-01T17:08:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep.8933/post-101959>

To add to the helpful advice...look up Buteyko breathing exercises....they will trigger your parasympathetic nervous system for relaxation which is helpful if your cortisol is wonky.

### healthman — 2013-12-04T18:30:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep.8933/post-102291>

Caroline - thank you for the reply - Yes I am a male  - I will become a detective and see if anything has changed in my environment - but I have not been doing anything different than usual. I will also see about arranging to eat earlier and take my supplements earlier to see if that makes a difference, my evening meal is not generally heavy (typical dinner would be steak, small portions of brocoli, asparagus, herbs, natto with butter) My meal size has decreased incredibly.
There are no emotional things going on at the moment everything in life is pretty much the same - that is why I found my NEW sleeping pattern weird?

Huck - Thank you for the reply - I do tend to try and relax as much as possible before I go to sleep but have never tried meditation or self hypnosis, my wife some evenings does reflexology on my feet which helps me relax and drift off to sleep (even though she is no expert it does help me relax) I will look in to your suggestions as anything is worth a try

Thanks SeaHorse - I will look Buteyko breathing up online and give that a go also

### caroline — 2013-12-04T23:31:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep.8933/post-102339>

Yesterday, in the bootcamp, Dr. Jackson talked about keeping a gratitude journal. Everynite write down 5 things you are grateful for. He said it does amazing things for DHEA levels ..... also meditation of course.

Do you not find steak heavy? What about trying some seafood for dinner? sashimi?

Do you wear a watch to bed? with a battery? =EMF

Here is my saliva tests and blood tests. Any analysis, info, insight and comments are really appreciated!!!!!

Some background info: have trouble putting on weight/muscle mass, wake up unrefreshed, wake up a few times a night roll over and then fall back to sleep, have no trouble getting to sleep though, acne/rosacea, feelings of being down, nearsightedness, trouble concentrating, some brain fog at times, difficulty regulating stress, eye floaters, sometimes i feel tired after eating.

### ozbuck — 2013-12-03T23:58:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ive-got-my-labs-id-appreciate-your-help.8952/post-102221>

Some info HoneyChild posted in my Journal:

"I still haven't had confirmation that I've calculated this correctly but I think your BUN/creatinine ratio is 18.77

S UREA 5.7 mmol/L (3.0-7.5) = 15.96 (BUN)
S CREAT. 75 umol/L (60-110) = 0.85

15.96/0.85 = 18.77

[http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitc...a\_nitrogen.php](http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitc...a_nitrogen.php)
[http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitc...Creatinine.php](http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitc...Creatinine.php)

If this is correct then you're dehydrated as your result is above 15."

and

"**RDW 14.4 (11-17)**

"Red cell distribution width or RDW is a parameter that measures variation in red blood cell size or red blood cell volume. A high RDW (over 14.5%) means that the red blood cells vary a lot in size. A normal RDW is 11.6 to 14.6%, but researchers from the Intermountain Medical Center Heart Institute found that patients with a RDW level greater than or equal to 12.9% had an increased risk for depression."

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthrea...els-Depression](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthrea...els-Depression)

This can be caused by deficiencies in b12, folate, iron, selenium, vitamin E and possibly some other things."

### Gracelind — 2013-12-04T08:31:55+0200

Adding my thanks and appreciation to Caroline's.

I appreciate Dr Tim's connecting this info to the SNPs for enzymes in the Methylation Cycle. Adding this to our analyses from Genetic Genie and Sterling's App is invaluable.

### caroline — 2013-12-04T08:43:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heal-your-hormones-webinar-3.8962/post-102256>

I have to move my butt and imput my 23&me ....is it easy to do? My computer skills are so limited....

### Gracelind — 2013-12-04T09:18:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heal-your-hormones-webinar-3.8962/post-102261>

Happy is good!

It takes a couple of steps but isn't hard to do. Log in to 23 and me then click on your name upper right. In drop-down menu click on Browse Raw Data. Upper right click on Download. Answer your security question. Save the zip file to your computer.

Then go to [http://geneticgenie.com](http://geneticgenie.com) and scroll down to Methylation Analysis. Click on the link. On the next page locate your Genome file from 23 in the Browse to upload file box. You'll get a chart with your Methylation SNPs and suggestions of how to address specific mutations.

At [http://mthfrsupport.com](http://mthfrsupport.com) click on Sterling's App on the top menu bar, then Order Report. It does cost $20.00 USD and uses Paypal. The analysis is much more comprehensive--more SNPs-- but doesn't include suggestions for supplements or discussion of the SNPs. It's necessary to do a search for the SNPs not on the Genetic Genie list to figure out what they are.

Have fun!

### caroline — 2013-12-04T22:12:43+0200

# Thread: Cure for a cough--sleep on a glacier?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cough-sleep-on-a-glacier.8963/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2013-12-04T08:54:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cough-sleep-on-a-glacier.8963/post-102258>

John Muir must have been in exceptional physical condition to cover the difficult territory that he did. He's known for such things as climbing to the top of a tall pine to ride it's wild tossing in the winds of storms. The man was clearly cold-adapted! What caught my eye is his recovery from a long-term respiratory condition while walking and sleeping on a glacier. Also, his comment on his physical strength in lugging his heavy sled over the broken icy terrain.

From Travels in Alaska by John Muir Chapter XVIII
My Sled-Trip On the Muir Glacier [http://www.sierraclub.org/john\_muir\_exhibit/writings/travels\_in\_alaska/chapter\_18.aspx](http://www.sierraclub.org/john_muir_exhibit/writings/travels_in_alaska/chapter_18.aspx)

_I slept well last night in the middle of the icy sea \[on the Muir glacier\]. The wind was cold but my sleeping-bag enabled me to lie neither warm nor intolerably cold. **My three-months cough is gone. Strange that with such work and exposure one should know nothing of sore throats and of what are called colds. My heavy, thick-soled shoes, resoled just before starting on the trip six days ago, are about worn out and my feet have been wet every night. But no harm comes of it, nothing but good.**

I succeeded in getting a warm breakfast in bed. I reached over the edge of my sled, got hold of a small cedar stick that I had been carrying, whittled a lot of thin shavings from it, stored them on my breast, then set fire to a piece of paper in a shallow tin can, added a pinch of shavings, held the cup of water that always stood at my bedside over the tiny blaze with one hand, and fed the fire by adding little pinches of shavings until the water boiled, then pulling my bread sack within reach, made a good warm breakfast, cooked and eaten in bed.

Thus refreshed, I surveyed the wilderness of crevassed, hummocky ice and concluded to try to drag my little sled a mile or two farther, then, finding encouragement, persevered, getting it across innumerable crevasses and streams and around several lakes and over and through the midst of hummocks, and at length reached the western shore between five and six o'clock this evening, extremely fatigued. **This I consider a hard job well done, crossing so wildly broken a glacier, fifteen miles of it from Snow Dome Mountain, in two days with a sled weighing altogether not less than a hundred pounds.**_

### caroline — 2013-12-04T23:38:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cough-sleep-on-a-glacier.8963/post-102343>

another book I have to get...... that gives me goosebumps!

# Thread: An EMF/EMR treasure trove of information and measurement devices.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-emf-emr-treasure-trove-of-information-and-measurement-devices.8976/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CindyB — 2013-12-05T19:58:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-emf-emr-treasure-trove-of-information-and-measurement-devices.8976/post-102436>

I've been reading this paper:
Physics Â± Uspekhi 46 (3) 259 Â± 291 (2003)
"Effects of weak magnetic fields on biological systems: physical aspects
V N Binhi, A V Savin"
available here: [http://biomag.narod.ru/pdf/2003.Binhi.ea.PUsp.pdf](http://biomag.narod.ru/pdf/2003.Binhi.ea.PUsp.pdf)

"Unlike biomagnetism studying magnetic fields (MF) of biological systems \[1\], magnetobiology focuses on biological reactions and mechanisms of action of weak (below 1 mT) magnetic fields. There is rapidly growing interest in the biological action of weak magnetic and electromagnetic fields. The American periodical Microwave News has pub- lished a list of hundreds of the Internet hyperlinks to organizations directly involved in electromagnetobiological research (http://www.microwavenews.com/www.html)."

[http://microwavenews.com/about-us](http://microwavenews.com/about-us)

"For more than 30 years, Microwave News has been reporting on the potential health and environmental impacts of electromagnetic fields and radiation. We are widely recognized as a fair and objective source of information on this controversial subject.

Microwave News covers the entire non-ionizing electromagnetic spectrum, with special emphasis on mobile phones and power lines, as well as radar and broadcast towers.

The first print issue was published in January 1981. All our print issues may be downloaded from the News Center at no cost.

In June 2003, Microwave News converted to a Web-based format.

Microwave News is independent and is not aligned with any industry or government agency. Today, we rely on contributions from our readers."​

Check out their tabs for \[EMF/EMR Meters\] and \[EMF/EMR Directory\]. Info on organizations in lots of different countries.

I can see that Dr. J referenced this site back in April this year here:
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6773-My-City&p=84123&highlight=microwavenews#post84123](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6773-My-City&p=84123&highlight=microwavenews#post84123)

Good stuff.

### spalmi — 2013-12-06T17:40:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cryolipolysis.8984/post-102591>

In an interview I heard Jack mention Cryolipolysis. I have been reading about this process and it seems like it may be a completely different process that cold thermogenesis. According to what I read bellow 41 degrees fat cells are triggered to die, which they do immidiately, then an inflammation process takes place to elminate the waste.

### spalmi — 2013-12-11T12:51:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cryolipolysis.8984/post-103127>

I'll add why I suspect it's a different process.

Jack describes the process of cold thermo as decreasing inflammation, Cryolipolysis seems to increase inflammation.
I don't think Jack recommends temperatures cold enough to cause this process to happen.
People argue that using Water and Ice your fat cells don't get called enough for this to happen, they use a technology that pulls your fat away from your body to reduce the conflicting heating of the fad from your own body.

# Thread: Lower blood flow to stomach?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2013-12-08T12:36:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-102831>

Hi guys,

I have stomach issues from even simple things like plain water. My heart constantly feels like it is beating really hard and it is worse after food/water. I can also constantly see and feel my abdomen pulsating greatly in relation to my heart palpitations.

This could be my stomach causing problems (stress response) and sending more blood to my stomach. Or it could be that my stomach is struggling to get enough blood causing all my symptoms.

So my question is, could taking NSAIDs have caused permanently lower blood flow to my stomach which is causing my symptoms?

Tried googling it and didn't get much help at all.

Any help from you guys massively appreciated.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-14T13:17:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103397>

Shameless bump.

Would a damaged vagus nerve cause lower blood flow. Could this cause symptoms such as feeling sickly in proportion to amount of volume un stomach, feeling food/liquid rise back up through throat, belching long after meals, etc?

### nonchalant — 2013-12-14T17:24:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103405>

Seems to make sense that nerve damage could cause stomach problems, Sean. I've been reading one of Becker's books, and one thing he discovered is that for the animal's leg to regenerate, it needs the nerve to be in the stump. If the blood vessels were all intact, but he removed the nerve back to the backbone, the leg wouldn't regenerate. The Schwann cells provided the energy needed.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-14T19:28:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103411>

Very interesting. Do you know of any tests that could confirm this? Any potential remedies?

### nonchalant — 2013-12-14T19:55:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103414>

Not me, no. Do you know of any issues with your Vagus nerve or other nerves? Perhaps your back, any issues with the T6 or nearby vertebrae?

### seanb4 — 2013-12-14T20:10:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103415>

Not that I know of. The only connection I can make is when I took the NSAIDs 2yrs ago maybe this damaged the vagus nerve. This was when all my problems started. But I have no idea how to confirm this or fix this if that is the case.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23and-me-shackled-by-our-up-govt.9006/post-102875>

I was that in an email that I got from them. I am so glad that I bought mine last month, like on the 17th but I am still waiting for the results. This is just big government not wanting to let people be responsible for their own health and rely on the government to tell them what the should and should not do.

From the email I got.

"Dear 23andMe Customers,

I'm writing to update you on our conversation with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and how it impacts you.

If you are a customer whose kit was purchased before November 22, 2013, your 23andMe experience will not change. You will be able to access both ancestry and health-related information as you always have.

23andMe has complied with the FDA's directive and stopped offering new consumers access to health-related genetic results while the company moves forward with the agency's regulatory review processes. Be sure to refer to our 23andMe blog for updates.

We stand behind the data we have generated for customers. Our lab partner adheres to strict quality standards that are part of the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments of 1988 - known as CLIA. These are the same standards used in the majority of other health and disease-related tests.

You are among the first people in the world to ever get access to their genomes. You are genetic pioneers. Thank you for your ongoing support and we look forward to continuing to serve you.

Anne Wojcicki
Co-founder and CEO, 23andMe "

### Destiny — 2013-12-09T00:48:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23and-me-shackled-by-our-up-govt.9006/post-102926>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=102842)
>
> so I asked my Mom for 23and me test for Christmas. Just received the kit with an offer for refund because they can only provide ancestral info and no medical due to FDA.WTFWTFWTFWTFWTFWTFWTF???????????? Talk about cortisol through the roof. bad enough I would have had to drive out of NY state to mail the kit. Now we can not get our own genetics. I have nothing to add at this point except expletives. there is no reasonable response to this violation of human rights.

I share your frustration, Dali Dula. I got one kit on 11/26 for a relative. The big government wants to be in control of us for whatever... They don't want you to be in a charge of your own health. They might let you maybe a little and only when they fully supervise you.

### TheKid — 2013-12-09T13:14:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23and-me-shackled-by-our-up-govt.9006/post-102960>

I think the crux of the whole matter is that the government wants to find a way to preempt patient legal action. What do I mean? Well, think about how many times you've been to a doctor, complained about some health issue, and then had the doctor say, "It's probably something genetic. We don't really know why this happens. But drugX can treat this condiditon."

So the government needs 23andMe to market itself as being less than reliable and less than diagnostic so that raft of lawsuits don't erupt when docs say stuff like that.

# Thread: Optimal Treatment of Hashimoto's Disease - Searching for the unifying theory!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-treatment-of-hashimotos-disease-searching-for-the-unifying-theory.9043/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Arsaneus — 2013-12-11T18:15:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-treatment-of-hashimotos-disease-searching-for-the-unifying-theory.9043/post-103138>

Hello folks,

since I'm new here, I'd like to give you all an overview about me, before I get on with my question. Please read everything since I think many of this will be totally new ideas that you've never read in this form anywhere before. Thanks!

**My story so far**
I've been experiencing hypothyroid symptoms since about 2006, not knowing where they came from. At first, I ignored them, but in the end of 2011 they became so obvious (12 to 15 hours of sleep every day), that I finally went to the doctor's. They all told me I was fine. I've been diagnosed with Hashimoto's Thyreoiditis in April 2012 by my own effort of measuring Thyreoglobuline antibodies (TG-AB), which were the only definite clue that I have this disease at all. Other antibodies (Thyreoperoxidase aka TPO-AB, and TSH receptor antibodies) were negative on several occasions. Ultrasound is completely normal with a thyroid volume of approximately 15 mL total. No unusual echo patterns or anything like that at all, I've seen it with my own eyes.

The TG-AB were the only clue to what's causing my symptoms, they were about three times above the normal range. My mother also has Hashimoto's Thyreoiditis but didn't told me. Since the antibodies convinced one of the doctors to help me, I got a prescription of Levothyroxine. I started with 25 mcg of T4 and increased the dosage in 4 to 6 week intervals, until my TSH suppressed. I therefore went through the whole range of possible dosages, from being hypothyroid to being hyperthyroid, without any significant effect on my symptoms - besides, that I only needed to shower two times a day with boiling hot water instead of three times, and I only slept for 10 hours instead of 12 to 15 hours.

I also had an alarming amount of micronutritient deficites, which of course no doctor believed until I paid for the necessary lab tests myself and showed them the results made in "their" own lab. Needless to say, that I had to treat them myself.

Additionally, I had massive gastrointestinal problems. My bowel was bulged like a fat man (although I'm 1,81m tall, my weight is 63kg and my body fat is about 7%, no matter how much I eat, that's been normal for me all my life by the way). But of course, nobody believed me because a skinny person like me with a swallen abdomen just looks thin and skinny anyway to every doctor. I couldn't eat any tiny amount of lactose or fructose, and even histamine was problematic. I felt like my whole body just stopped functioning and was falling apart.

I later found out by myself that I've been suffering from gluten intolerance (not celiac disease! this was ruled out very consequently via antibody tests and gastroduodenoscopy!) all the time, which has actually been approved to be a "real" disease a few years ago, but cannot be measured by any lab test. With eliminating gluten, my symptoms got better within 4 weeks, and were almost gone after 8 weeks. By accident, I discovered that soy also causes problems for me. By eliminating both, gluten and soy, I can now eat everything I want again, except these two of course. Nobody told me that either, in fact both doctors and internet communities constantly tried to discourage me on my way to finding out what really is the underlying problem... .

With nothing left but the thyroid disease, I now try to figure out how to treat this, so that all of my symptoms disappear completely. This has been particularly annoing since everybody, both doctors and internet communities, tell you a different story everytime, but always based upon observations for themselves only, and (almost) never based upon scientifically proven facts or scientifically plausible hypothesis/ideas. Needless to say, that I had to fight my own way through this again.

After reading much, and by this I mean people's stories and scientific publications as well, and some testing of ideas by myself and some friends (in a German Hashimoto community), I came finally closer and closer to the solution. Then, I noticed an odd case of well-being in this forum, which seems to prove one of my theories. This is why I'm here, and what I want to talk to you about.

**What I've accomplished so far**
Without having to go into too much details, I can say that I managed to eliminate most of the problems that the usual methods of substitution thyroid hormones carry within. I'm only sleeping for 7 to 9 hours a day, I'm finally having good grades at university again, and I even started to do sports again three times a week (by sports I mean heavy lifting in the gym, because I obviously want to gain some weight). This is what is possible to me now without the need to suppress my TSH. Yeah, you read that correctly.

I currently use a physiologic approach, trying to mimic the function of a healthy thyroid gland, by using a combination therapy of synthetic T3+T4. That's the most brief description I can give. The basic tricks are to take T4 two times a day (directly after waking up, without eating for the next 30 min. - and directly before going to bed, without eating 2 hours before), and to take T3 four times a day in intervals of approximately 5 hours. Both is designed to minimizing peaks in the fT3 and fT4, and to optimize equal supply of thyroid hormones throughout 24 hours a day. Another important trick is to choose the total dosage wisely, so hat TSH is not suppressed; there are scientifically proven TSH receptors outside (!) the thyroid gland and pituitary gland, which no one knows of what they're good for, and I don't want to shut the TSH down unless we're absolutely sure that it wouldn't affect any of the body's other functions. Besides, this way the body can produce new thyroid hormones even in between two dosages one takes. Believe me, I feel like a new man since I started that method, although I still have a lot of symptoms and I still don't feel completely normal/healthy.

I tracked down the problem to an inadequate restoration of euthyreosis on a cellular level. Although TSH, fT3 and fT4 are normal, and the body is therefore euthyroid on a systemic level, the cells still seem to be hypothyroid. Of course, I read a lot about rT3 and the way it influences cells (and got a heart attack when I saw the so called "calculations" of the fT3/rT3 ratio ... man, did anyone ever use units when calculating this? Got a new approach on this as well). But I couldn't help but wonder: Why would the pituitary secrete a normal amount of TSH when the cells are still hypothyroid due to too much rT3?

The answer: Because rT3 does not influence the pituitary TSH secretion at all, as it has been proven with rats! _(Cettour-Rose et al. - Inhibition of pituitary type 2 deiodinase by reverse triiodothyronine does not alter thyroxine-induced inhibition of thyrotropin secretion in hypothyroid rats, [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16131606](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16131606))_

We seem to have two independently operating systems of thyroid regulation in the body: The thyreotropic regulation system (the pituitary regulating the amount of TSH secreted, by measuring the amount of free T3 and free T4 in the bloodstream) + the competitive inhibition system (rT3 competing with T3 about the T3-receptors). Since the study proves that rT3 has no effect on TSH, both systems are independantly regulating the thyroid status of every cell in the body. Probably designed as one system being a slow, long-term regulation system and the other being a fast, quick-response system.

Three people (two members of our internet community + myself) tried the already described substitution method with a T3:T4 ratio in the dosage that was identical to what the healthy thyroid gland produces, approximately 1:14 (T4:T3) if you take the resorption in the jejunum into account (approx. 75% of orally given T4 being resorbed, but approx. 95% of orally given T3) and use molar unity (molecules, not micrograms, since T3 and T4 have a different molecular weight).

What did we find out?

A team of physicists has provided some of the clearest evidence yet that our Universe could be just one big projection.

Nevertheless, says Maldacena, the numerical proof that these two seemingly disparate worlds are actually identical gives hope that the gravitational properties of our Universe can one day be explained by a simpler cosmos purely in terms of quantum theory.

# Thread: December 2013 webinar..........

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5

### caroline — 2013-12-14T01:34:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/december-2013-webinar.9078/post-103364>

This is a very provocative webinar.......

I think we see a different side to Dr. K. - at least a little... A very thoughtful,introspective man, who in his heart, just wants life to be better for all of us......and that is why he pounds on us. We need to think more and ask better questions. I think those of us who have been following along for three years always knew that - but it was nice to see a softer side.......

so many thoughts......
a grand unified force
life responds to changes in environment
co-evolution
epigenetic shifts
information sensing
and on and on and on ..... I need to listen a few more times.

All of this - "The framework" has been in his head for 10 years and he is sharing with us everything going on in there!

I didn't realise that Einstein was only 19, with no physics background ......

I have so much to learn......

Thanks Dr. K.

### caroline — 2013-12-14T01:40:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/december-2013-webinar.9078/post-103365>

Dr. K. said he is taking a break over Christmas .... giving us a chance to catch up and absorb all of this! Thank the good Lord for that  ..... and I think maybe he has to turn down his thinking machine for awhile and have lots of fun with his family and be silly and eccentric and peculiar ..... just saying ....

### Inger — 2013-12-14T08:11:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/december-2013-webinar.9078/post-103394>

Love this Audrey. The webinar was actually very moving. I watched it yesterday evening... and in the evening my brain is not at its sharpest and I am very far from a geek so lots of stuff sailed past my brain...huh but I got so much out of it anyways We are all the N1 and each of us is so much needed to make a change! It is so important every step and choice that we do. The world desperately needs starfish -> people who have successfully healed and helped themselves out from the darkness... and pay it forward.
People who are centered! This really resonated with me when he talked about that. to be centered is such a beautiful thing! And something, I guess, many miss today.. and that is also why they are not able to make good choices. I think the icedip in the river always makes me very centered ? Negative entropy. What a beautiful word to me now! I have been thinking about it quite a bot last days and it so resonates with my thoughts and feelings about stuff. Negative entropy and being centered kind of belong together in my weird mind

From Sally today on the blogcomments.. I just loved it!

> Some notes: Does CT help us make galaxies in the universe of our cells? Free energy is a good thing.
>
> The term 'Negative entropy' is needed because energy can both increase or decrease entropy.
>
> Evolution doesn't ask "Is your refrigerator running?" Instead it asks "Is your refrigerator efficient?"

Efficiency truly is something beautiful. Wow... I so love where we are going!!!! It is exactly what I need! Wow... I cannot even say how much Jack has helped me in my life, if he only knew!

On another note - went to bed at 9:30 PM yesterday, today morning I woke up at 5 AM in my freezing bedroom, because I was BOILING of oxytocin.. haha I so much wanted the dream to continue too bad it was so intense I woke up! wow. I just LOVE it! I wonder if I have to thank Jack for this
Must be some quantum hormones at play? Maybe I got some from Jacks, as he has plenty? haha... IDK but I think this life is just so amazing and what a adventure!!!

### tellmisty — 2013-12-15T04:08:37+0200

### caroline — 2013-12-15T04:24:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/december-2013-webinar.9078/post-103473>

I have just re listened - Dr. K. really explains so many things in easy to understand terms ....This is an awesome webinar!

He says several times that we don't have to get all of this - all the difficult physics ... but we do have to understand the systems that apply to each of us. He very patiently explains a lot in terms that us non-geeks can understand.

There is a huge amount in this webinar ......I got so much more out of it the second time.

Thanks again Dr. K.

[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B004YWHXKU/ref=cm\_cr\_ryp\_prd\_ttl\_sol\_0](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B004YWHXKU/ref=cm_cr_ryp_prd_ttl_sol_0)
Wonder if they are blue blocking like the other UVEX glasses, like the UV Extreme ones.

I also like canned cod livers as a gift: [http://www.amazon.com/King-Oscar-Liver-6-67-Ounces-Tins/dp/B002147OIO/ref=cm\_cr\_pr\_product\_top](http://www.amazon.com/King-Oscar-Liver-6-67-Ounces-Tins/dp/B002147OIO/ref=cm_cr_pr_product_top)

### caroline — 2013-12-15T00:38:23+0200

cod livers are a great idea - I have forgotten about those

Sally - fo you know about the different colours of lenses? do these block blue lite?

### nonchalant — 2013-12-15T00:42:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-inspired-christmas-list.9084/post-103441>

I have two UVEX orange glasses. Both block blue light beautifully. But they also have the "UV Extreme" phrase as part of their names. And they are a lot cheaper. I suppose because these are new. I may have to wait for the dust to settle a bit before grabbing a pair. Perhaps soon there will be a review or two.

Just because you see orange lenses, doesn't mean they are what you need. They need to make a blue LED light, or the blue and purple parts of a rainbow disappear.

### caroline — 2013-12-15T00:56:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruse-inspired-christmas-list.9084/post-103443>

I spent a lot of time at my eye glass place going over this .... that is exactly right - just because they are yellow or orange doesn't mean they block blue light.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-dum-way-to-roast-your-turkey.9115/post-103587>

Came across this flipping through a paper catalog.

We worked for two years to create the first Wi-Fi-enabled leave-in probe thermometer for your kitchen. Connect our smart thermometer via our app for iPhone, iPad or iPod Touch (and coming in 2014, for Android), and it keeps you posted on an array of cooking details anywhere within range of your Wi-Fi connectionâ€”a revolutionary way to take the guesswork out of a perfectly cooked meal. Plan and track your entire mealâ€”one thermometer does it all through one-screen monitoring of temperature, cooking progress and time remaining. Schedule separate timers for side dishes and alerts for tasks like basting the turkey so everything gets to the table on time.

~Makes me wanna puke....

# Thread: Grounding questions.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2013-12-16T18:36:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103600>

Hi guys.

I have been reading up on EMFs and grounding and am rather confused.

1\. If I ground via a steak in the ground outside leading to a grounding mat on my bed, can dirty electricity jump onto this mat and lead to extra electricity going through me? If so this would be counter productive, yes.

2\. Can you test for dirty electricity by using a multimeter? If your meter reads zero volts can there still be dirty electricity present.

3\. If using a grounding mat lowers my body voltage is this good regardless or is there hidden dangers that could make it bad?

Thanks in advance!

### nonchalant — 2013-12-17T01:41:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103628>

Yeah, I understand your confusion. I used to sleep on a grounded sheet (to the earth outside) and it did wonders for my back. But after my back improved, I started getting some inflammatory issues that caused me to stop using the sheet. If you want to prevent the sheet acting like an antenna for artificial EMF, you could wrap it around you.
I still ground myself (ankle strap connected to stake in the ground) when I take ice baths; I've found that it increases my ability to comfortably tolerate the cold.

If my back were to get in the same condition it was a year or two ago, I would start using the grounding sheet at night right away.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-17T10:20:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103652>

So the problem with grounding/earthing mats is that the larger surface area picks up dirty electricity, were as a grounding strap has a smaller surface area so this doesn't happen?

You say you got inflammatory issues. How do you know this? Did you get tests or was it just symptoms? What symptoms?

### Tanya — 2013-12-17T16:22:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103661>

seanb4, I am new to this website and not an expert in any of this, but I can share with you my experience with the grounding. Please pardon my English first as it is not my first language. I’ve been using a combination of Magnetico matress, grounding bed sheet, and several grounding pads and straps - all of them connected to the outside of my house to combat my RA. After seeing a discussion here about the possibility of exposing myself to a dirty EMF, I immediately removed the grounding sheet from my bed, but my inflammation and pain increased significantly only after just one week being without it. I put it back again and measured myself with a body voltage meter. The results of that testing left no doubt for me that I definitely benefit a lot from doing the grounding the way I was doing it before. Maybe that is what you need to do yourself - test it and see how you react. Hope this helps.

### nonchalant — 2013-12-17T16:39:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103665>

Tanya, that is great advice, checking with a body voltage meter. Everyone is at a different health level, and everyone's environment is different. Plus in QED nothing can be taken for granted.
I also removed my sheet when the "antenna issue" first arose, but after just a few days, I replaced the sheet.

Sean, I started getting edema, and that was when I stopped with the grounding sheet. I also stopped using magnets during the day. Fortunately for me, my back had healed nicely, and I didn't miss the sheet at that (later) time. I think the grounding strap for me is good because I only use it for an hour. I also use magnets during CT, but again, only for an hour. I wouldn't remove my Magnetico pad for anything, on the other hand.

I do miss my daytime magnets, however. My tight throat has been creeping back. Need. to. leave. city.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-17T17:06:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103668>

Thanks for the information Tanya and Nonchalant.

Okay to clarify:
If your body voltage goes down when you ground then it is beneficial compared to no grounding at all?
If there is dirty electricity then does this show as a rise in body voltage when "grounding" or can there be damaging dirty electricity even though your body voltage drops? Can there be dirty electricity at 0V?

Also I would like to try to get free electrons from magnets but the magnetico pads are way too expensive for me. Any cheap alternatives?

### Tanya — 2013-12-17T18:50:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103673>

Sean, I’ll give it another try, but I hope that someone who is more knowledgeable will chime in. After I saw my body voltage drop to the ideal number while being grounded, I assumed that there was no dirty electricity issue to worry about at that point. As far as the magnetico pad - it is the best one there is. You can definitely find some cheaper ones, but the question is if you will get as much benefit from them as from magnetico. Magnetico’s price went up a lot since I purchased mine a few years ago... I understand how it might be difficult to be able to afford one right now. I saw a post somewhere on this site mentioning a discount arrangement with Dr. Kruse - I don’t know if it is still active.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-17T23:11:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103687>

Okay, yeah even with discount it would be too much. I think I read somewhere that putting two magnets under your bed has a similar effect...

### seanb4 — 2013-12-19T17:55:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103850>

Okay so I got my voltmeter today and I measure at roughly 4v in my bed room. When I use my grounding strap, that's connected to earth outside, it drops to 0.6v. So as long as there are no random spikes, does this mean I don't have to worry about dirty electricity and I should use the grounding strap without negative consequences?

### nonchalant — 2013-12-19T20:26:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103855>

Sean, there are no guarantees where EMF is concerned. Glad you got the meter, it will help you figure out your environment.
I personally think the grounding strap will help, though you might not notice the difference during sleep. If you use it during CT, you might notice a nice difference right away.

### JoeBranca — 2013-12-19T20:29:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103856>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=103850)
>
> Okay so I got my voltmeter today and I measure at roughly 4v in my bed room. When I use my grounding strap, that's connected to earth outside, it drops to 0.6v. So as long as there are no random spikes, does this mean I don't have to worry about dirty electricity and I should use the grounding strap without negative consequences?

It's something I've been wondering ... does grounding oneself to reduce body voltage necessarily mean an improvement in the situation? If I understand correctly, once you ground yourself, using the body voltmeter method you're now measuring the difference between something grounded and something grounded, so by definition yes it should be very close to zero if it's sufficient grounding. However, if you had a positive body voltage to begin with, once you ground yourself you now have an electric current, which should be measured differently than with a voltmeter (ampmeter?). Or, perhaps body voltages would measure better in that case across head to toe. Unsure how to go about that for an accurate picture.

Spending more money and focus on eliminating or reducing the sources of the electric fields (or ground them instead of oneself) that are producing significant body voltages in the first place seems like a safer approach.

### nonchalant — 2013-12-19T20:37:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103858>

Good questions, Joe. I ground myself (other than walking barefoot) only a little in the city now. When I'm in the country, however, I sleep with a grounding sheet every night. And the yard around the house out there is getting very clean of stickers, cactus, etc. Nice to be able to walk confidently!

I think everyone would have to try grounding out to see if it helped them or not. If you have some serious issues, like I had with my back, I personally think I would definitely try it.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-19T20:41:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103860>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=103855)
>
> Sean, there are no guarantees where EMF is concerned. Glad you got the meter, it will help you figure out your environment.
> I personally think the grounding strap will help, though you might not notice the difference during sleep. If you use it during CT, you might notice a nice difference right away.

Okay so how do I know if it is beneficial or not? If lowering voltage is no guarantee then what test can you do to be sure?

### Tanya — 2013-12-19T21:00:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103862>

Sean, this is how I understand this as well. Frankly, the voltmeter basically confirmed what I already discovered prior to testing with it - not being grounded for a week was making it worse for me. That alone was enough for me.

### Tanya — 2013-12-19T21:17:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103864>

> [JoeBranca said:](/goto/post?id=103856)
>
> Spending more money and focus on eliminating or reducing the sources of the electric fields (or ground them instead of oneself) that are producing significant body voltages in the first place seems like a safer approach.

Joe, that is a very good advice and I try to do as much as I can, but obviously you can only do so much if there things that you can not control yourself. That is when the grounding sheet and pads come into play for me.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-20T00:06:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-103872>

It is good advise to limit as much as you can but like Tanya says, you can only control so much. I just want to know that I'm not making my situation worse by grounding myself...

### Inger — 2013-12-22T11:31:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-104034>

Sean I have the same issue with my grounding rod. Not sure if it is good or not! After my latest AM radio measurements I am even more unsure.... But I know how to get the answer. We need to make a study, and that is; Buy some flowers, put half of them in a glass and the other half in another glass. Same glass same water. Put them close to another on the same place/in same room (but not touching) and put your grounding rod in one of the glasses with flowers. Then wait and see which one will last longer! If the grounding rod flower glass will last longer we know it will be a good thing

I have wanted to do this many times already but every time I bought flowers they went to something else! 1st time it was fathers day... and the yellow roses I have bought for my experiment just looked so pretty on the fathers day table so I just had to give them away! Next time I bought some red roses and they looked too pretty to just keep in the bedroom for an experiment so there it went again.... I need to get more disciplined!!! I am just too addicted to beauty in my life

### seanb4 — 2013-12-22T19:41:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-104050>

That would be very interesting to do but there would be other variables that would affect it like the quality of the seed, and other stuff...

### Inger — 2013-12-22T20:14:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-104053>

No other stuff Sean! Buy flowers! Like roses or other flowers, I bought 10 pieces for 3 €, they have those great offers once in a while. You could do 10 and 10. The roses all look the same, full grown, no seeds! You must take care the roses are close and same light, temp and same room. Easy!

### Chiquita — 2014-01-06T04:33:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-104857>

You guys can ground to a water faucet in your house and get the same benefit as driving a stake in the ground outside. Water lines are grounded. You also get grounded every time you take a shower. The stream from the shower head and the water you are standing in is grounded. I believe all this grounding thing is a power of suggestion thing.

### seanb4 — 2014-01-06T09:59:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-104859>

Grounding via a water faucet is good to know. Could be useful. Thanks Chiquita.

### Inger — 2014-01-09T14:17:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-105148>

Ok Sean, I did the flower + grounding rod experiment and the results are clear to me now! I put 12 white roses in two glasses of water, 6 and 6. In one I put my grounding rod in the glass/water. I had them in m y bedroom 5 cm from each other so they would not touch.
Now after two weeks or so the one with the grounding rod is fresher looking, no flowers are brown, and the leaves are way fresher too. The other non grounded glass have 2 roses that are pretty dead, brown and hanging and also the green/leaves are hanging and the other roses too look less fresh. It is pretty amazing what difference! Not huge as I do not want to wait longer (because I want to use the grounding rod myself lol), but very clear! I am pretty sure I had exactly the same amount of water in them to begin with, and the grounded glass have about 1,5 cm more water now.... like it used less water? (But both glasses do still have are more than half full with water to make sure they will not die from too little water) But I need to redo the experiment to be sure.
I will repeat the experiment (I think as it gets warmer, this spring. because it gets cold now and the roses + the water might freeze in my bedroom) and then I will measure the water too exactly, and then I will put a copper rod in the non grounded glass too, just to check that it is not the copper that preserves them. I doubt so, but that would make the experiment even better.

So from tonight I use my grounding rod again with no doubts =)

Iif you have tested yours with a similar experiment and what come out, let me know, ok!

### seanb4 — 2014-01-09T14:33:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-105149>

Sounds very promising. Good work! Will definitely look forward to the follow up experiment...take photos.

Yeah I've stopped using my ground wire the last couple days as the wire keeps braking, I need to buy a thicker one. Anyway I have a headache today, although I did drink more water yesterday, 3L, so could be to do with that or something else.

How well does your rod ground you? My current set-up only gets me to roughly 0.3V I think. I'll remeasure when I use a thicker wire.

### Inger — 2014-01-09T14:44:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-105152>

I do not have stuff to measure that with Sean. But I have a pretty thick rod! The thickest I got, there are many many thin copper wires in it. It goes to the river/waterfall... it is maybe 20 meters long or so. In the river I have a real grounding rod tied to the one that I use for grounding rod. It is rubber/plastic around the long rod but I have peeled it so I can tie it around my ankle. I also have a grounding strap, I sometimes use that it fells a bit more comfortable that the copper rod to the skin.

I need to get the stuff you are measuring with! Would be interesting to know.

### seanb4 — 2014-01-09T15:01:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-questions.9117/post-105154>

Sounds like a good set-up you've got there. I bought a multi meter from ebay, was like Â£20. You've got to make sure it can measure ac voltage in millivolts or something. Basic multimeter's aren't accurate enough for what we need.
