### lubylouUK — 2014-03-03T11:27:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-sleep.9377/post-113150>

Hello, I'm new here. Hope you don't mind me butting in. I have adrenal exhaustion with horrendous sleep problems since the birth of my daughter. It's got a lot better but I've struggled with high cortisol at night and I found that the Tryptophan would make my heart race at night. Have you tried giving up the HTP/Tryptophan? Seratonin increase causes the adrenals to stress to keep up with the higher energy it produces. THis is not exact but you get my drift?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-03T12:06:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-sleep.9377/post-113153>

> [margor\_j said:](/goto/post?id=113144)
>
> ...what if its affecting you and you dont know!

I've thought the same as you, margor.

AFAICT, the only comprehensive tests for gluten intolerance (and possibly casein intolerance, too) are the Cyrex ones. People who should know, like Tom O'Bryan, seem to think so. And, as we now know, problems people get from gluten can be much more subtle than a bellyache following immediately on from eating a piece of toast. Indeed, it sound like they usually are. This is the problem: there might be no immediate and obvious connection to be made in terms of symptoms and a food.

Since I'm not in the U.S. and can't get good antibody testing, it seems to me that the only safe thing is to _assume_ sensitivity on the foods that commonly are problematic. This obviously means gluten-containing foods, but it seems that casein often turns up as "cross-reactive" once people have a problem with gluten even if they might not have had any problems had gluten never been in the diet. I've thought it best to knock all dairy on the head except clarified butter. I compromised with butter and cream and cheese but not milk, then switched to only cheese and butter from goat's and ewe's milk. In the end, it just seemed better to go the whole hog.

### sooperb — 2014-03-03T12:48:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-sleep.9377/post-113160>

Pigs milk Mike? Novel

### caroline — 2014-01-19T00:03:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/without-adequate-sleep-sanna-said-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C5%93you-get-sick-fat-and-stupid-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%EF%BF%BE.9385/post-106152>

I always thought I was bullet proof ... didn't need hardly any sleep - for years and years.... how dopey can one be????

### nicld — 2014-01-19T01:07:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/without-adequate-sleep-sanna-said-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C5%93you-get-sick-fat-and-stupid-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%EF%BF%BE.9385/post-106157>

Yep, for years and years I always said that sleep was over rated as I worked nights, went to college, worked out and raised a family (at the same time). Boy was I wrong and I am dealing with the effects now, 9 years later.

### Da-mo — 2014-01-19T01:21:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/without-adequate-sleep-sanna-said-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C5%93you-get-sick-fat-and-stupid-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%EF%BF%BE.9385/post-106160>

I have been a shiftworker for 20 years - and it did just that - made me sick, fat and stupid. The effects didnt show until 10 years in at age 35 where I started gaining weight and getting flu each winter. By year 15 at age 40 my brain was starting to go - loss of concentration, general fogginess etc. Now at 45 and having started taking care of myself again about 10 months ago, the fat is gone, I dont get sick and the mind fog is clearing.

The tough ones are refrigerators and stoves. I remember Nick saying that there was an old fridge on the other sides of his son's bedroom wall and it had very high EMF. His son was very ill .....

### caroline — 2014-01-21T02:58:05+0200

### Gracelind — 2014-01-21T07:59:36+0200

### Hope — 2014-01-21T23:03:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/washer-drier-near-the-bedroom.9401/post-106506>

> [Gracelind said:](/goto/post?id=106429)

Yep, I know. It's what helped cause mold in our current house. No good. I did send out for a HERTSMI2 on this possible rental.

Audrey, yes I am on a mission. I am certain people would think I am crazy. I FINALLY started using Michael's body voltage meter and wow. I should sleep in the kitchen or living room. Not the bedroom. And not near the windows. It is so weird; something must be going on in the wiring in the wall. Going to bring it with me to any new house we see. Though the realtor is already getting fed up after going out with us twice (we are renting, still). But I don't care. I have lost WAY too much of my health for mold and this wifi hell.

Just so thankful for all I'm learning here....no matter what.

And yes, my dream is to live in the middle of nowhere. I wish I could convince DH of that. But, I have actually thought of renting a small place in the mountains somewhere, on a temporary basis, to go heal, just see DH on weekends for some time. I feel SO different there. And then today with those body voltage meter readings...whoa. Anyway I digress.

But I do agree with the commune. If I were wealthy, I'd start a healing community for people to come. No wifi, as little electricity as possible, magneticos in all the beds. Though I have to research what Dr K said last night....so hope I can hear the recap. Gotta listen again to what he said to do if you're dehydrated...redox potential shot. Can't wait. I am dehydrated still I am sure.

# Thread: Is it what you eat or what you don't eat?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-what-you-eat-or-what-you-dont-eat.9408/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-21T09:20:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-what-you-eat-or-what-you-dont-eat.9408/post-106433>

I think it's both. Not eating poisonous plants or cadmium-concentrating polar bear liver, not to mention more everyday fare like gluten-containing grains and HFCS, is obviously necessary. And I think high quality, naturally raised food is the best thing to eat.

But I was thinking specifically of a remark Mike Eades made about terry Wahls' remarkable recovery. Now, she recommends, IIRC, two cups of cruciferous vegetables, two cups of red and orange vegetables, and two cups of sulphur-containg vegetables a day. Mike Eades gave her TED talk a very favourable review. Afterwards, someone popped up in his comments and said, what many people must have been thinking, that's an awful lot of vegetables to chew your way through. Dr. Eades replied with something along the lines of: Yes, it is; but is it what she's eating that counts or what she's not eating?

I thought of this later when I found that the ex-Polish Army doctor Jan Kwasnieski had claimed some success with treating multiple sclerosis with a low-carb diet. Nothing special in the way of seeking out antioxidants and plant phyto-nutrients or anything, mostly just prescribing a diet that, if memory serves, works out to about 80% fat, 13% protein, 7% carbohydrate. Now if there are any academic journal articles on this I guess they'll be in Polish and most of us couldn't read 'em. Interesting, though.

I thought of it again when reading David Perlmutter's new book _Grain Brain_. Perlmutter says that taking antioxidants doesn't actually get you very far and that the point is to switch on your own body's antioxidants. Maybe this is relevant.

I'm not knocking deeply-coloured vegetables, etc. and I do eat them -- though not in enormous quantities. But maybe it's the getting gluten and excessive carbohydrate out your diet that makes most of the difference. And I'm inclined to think there'd be little or no point in, say, swallowing vitamin C capsules if you're still, as it were, throwing paraffin on the fire.

It makes me think about those Nutrient Density tables again, too -- not for the first time. So what if you're "over the line" for this or that if your diet is basically unbalanced? Just "enough" C from all that fruit juice but eleven servings of grains a day on top (thank you USDA!). And this is beside the problem Jack has often pointed out that those tables ignore the special importance of brain-specific nutrients.

### caroline — 2014-01-21T09:53:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-what-you-eat-or-what-you-dont-eat.9408/post-106437>

Interesting thoughts .... that is a ton of veggies!

### Da-mo — 2014-01-21T10:40:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-what-you-eat-or-what-you-dont-eat.9408/post-106444>

\>..and dont forget "WHEN you eat". JK has given that at least as much importance.

Then today I was reading EE3 and 4 while trying to help a lady with AI, and find that eating clean would help but not fix the cause of the problem, which is EMF and its affects on Calcium and Magnesium. In this case, if I understand correctly, even trying to correct the Ca and Mg through the diet would not help as it would not be able to be utilised until the cause was removed.

# Thread: Veterinary help
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Linz — 2014-01-21T14:37:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106461>

I would welcome suggestions that might be worth exploring to help a young horse just diagnosed with one-sided temperohyoid osteoarthritis - a degenerative arthritic condition affecting the small bones that are part of vestibular system in head. Recommended treatment is to remove a small section of bone to prevent movement in the affected joint so that small bones don't fracture if they fuse which would lead to severe neuro damage. This does not seem to address the ongoing inflammation or bone damage but seems inevitable to prevent the severe consequence of continued movement.

The horse lives outside, unrugged, on a high fibre clean diet supplemented with seaweed, brewer's yeast, linseed, turmeric (and I can't really blame the mobile phone!).

I think something like [www.ice](http://www.ice) bonnet.com looks as though it might be a start. According to their website the horse is one of only two animals that don't have an internal system to cool blood going to the brain ( i haven't verified this anywhere else). Just wondering if this could be a reason to make it difficult to stop inflammation in the head without external help.

Homeopathic treatment over several months before diagnosis was not sufficient although I think there were some short term benefits, and Scenar treatment has helped another horse here with a fracture.

Have I missed any promising avenues to explore? Does anyone here have any knowledge of Boswellia as anti -inflammatory? It is being added to an increasing number of equine supplements now.

### SeaHorse — 2014-01-21T21:14:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106495>

You might want to look to see if somebody in your area does low photon light therapy, there are some "hats" made by Bioscan that work on the head area....just a thought. Serrapeptase is another possibility to help with damage from osteoarthritis. Precision Joint Solution is a supplement made in the US that a lot of people are having good experiences with....it has serrapeptase, HA, eggshell membrane and a few other things that you might be able to get individually...it is very pricey. Good luck.

### Linz — 2014-01-23T14:16:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106746>

Thank you SeaHorse,

The low photon light looks promising and is available here for a reasonable cost. I'm trying to get to grips with various supplements but do have to be careful with the cost. Lots of trouble with internet connnection here after high winds- just when I really need it.

I have delayed her surgery for a month to give me some time to try and reduce the inflammation first and I'm going to try to find out a bit more about mito support - mito research in horses seems to be very limited.

### HoneyChild — 2014-01-23T15:00:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106749>

I'm not 100% but I think Lynn Razaitis who runs the Iodine Workshop on Face Book takes borax for her arthritis and I think she's got a horse because her avatar is of a horse. I did a very quick search of the group and found this comment from her:-

"Our neighbors lab, who lived at our house half the time, started developing all kinds of hip problems and was miserable. I started giving her a secret morning potion of a little borax, liver powder, cod liver oil, collagen and raw egg. She perked up fast and was jumping around like a puppy. Neighbors were real excited to find something that worked."

### SeaHorse — 2014-01-23T15:09:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106751>

From what I've read about eggshell membrane it is loaded with collagen and all the components of a healthy joint, so it makes sense.

### Linz — 2014-01-25T14:53:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-107013>

Thank you both. I'm looking for inspiration to find the cause at the moment.
May go the route of remedial dentistry along with cranio sacral treatment

# Thread: Labs and relationship between cholesterol levels, hormones and sex hormone binding

### mamadell — 2014-01-21T19:32:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-and-relationship-between-cholesterol-levels-hormones-and-sex-hormone-binding.9419/post-106481>

new to this forum and to the paleo diet and way of life
just had a full lab report done and can't seem to find a doc in my area that can support me in coming into balance while addressing the cause and not the symptoms
i am 51 , in good shape, 118 lbs and am going thru menopause ...cycles are irregular
the lab values that concern me, are as follows :

total cholesterol 331
LDL cholesterol 239
HDL cholesterol 72
triglycerides 64
non HDL chol. ( LDL + VLDL ) 259
apoB100-calc 157
LDL-R ( real) 202
IDL cholesterol 28
Remnant Lipo ( IDL + VLDL3 ) 40

good thing is that my LDL are large buoyant

Hormone panel :
serum testosterone 27
free testosterone <0.2
progesterone 1.8
estradiol 222
dhea 196
total estrogens 391 HIGH
sex hormone binding globulin 162 HIGH

Thyroid panel :
TSH 1.21
T3 free serum 2.5
T4 free direct 1.51
reverse T3 16.1

Other values of interest :
glucose, serum 81
cortisol 10
prolactin 15.1
IGF-1 167
VIT.D 57.3
C-reactive Protein 0.11

i have had high cholesterol levels for 20 years and have noticed that the total levels are going up as i get older.
since entering menopause, my hormone levels go in and out of balance but the numbers on my sex hormone binding globulin keep going up ?
my i guess my liver is producing excess cholesterol and SHBG but why ?
currently i am following a paleo diet but before that i was mostly vegan
any insight would be greatly appreciated
thank you

SHBG - indicates how well you're clearing estrogens. looking at your estrogen/estridol you're not clearing estrogen properly. you probably also have some methylation issues.

am going thru menopause ...cycles are irregular
progesterone 1.8-----------may be low or ok, depends------------when was blood drawn in relation to day #1 of your cycle, how long was that cycle?
estradiol 222 ------------------------high
dhea 196 --------------------------low
total estrogens 391 -----------------high
sex hormone binding globulin 162 HIGH
cortisol 10---------------------------low (what time was blood drawn)
currently i am following a paleo diet but before that i was mostly vegan ------ go for (Epi-Paleo-RX) + (CT)+ be diligent about maintaining good Circadian Rhythm

> i just started taking BHRT but don't want to take , long term.. would rather try to balance my hormones, naturally.
> i've been told that i have some liver blood stagnation issues which would explain possible methylation issues..

There are limits when attempting natural hormonal balance, that mostly apply to hormones that correlate with circadian rhythm (cortisol, melatonin), most other steroid hormones you should manage for life.

BHRT, describe what are you taking
\==============================================================
DHEAs(Women 275-400) μg/dL
pregnenolone(<151)ng/dL

Easiest way to make some dent in high cholesterol would be to take pills and incerase pregnenolone and DHEAs to values close to the top ranges as above.
Any DHEA pills would do, but be consistent with manufacturer pills that you use.
My self I have good experience with pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix from Nutricology.

That may also increase your low cortisol and help with progesterone management during cycle.

You have very high SHBG. SHBG binds testosterone and estrogens. Will be hard to lower high estrogens.
OTOH, estrogens may go lower if you work on their excretion rate.
Eat cruciferous vegetables, add som calcium-D-glutarate.
Eventually you may need to use Arimidex (prescription drug, aromatase inhibitor), but that latter.

\-------------------------------------
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio)

Consider getting this two tests done, sooner, better:
scan each page, post it.

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel
[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)
Price: $350.00

At labCorp or Mayo Clinic
Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum
insurance should pay for this test.

//////////////
check

homocysteine
B12
B6
Folic Acid

The last three are part of Spectracell, may skip in blood testing.
////////

Do not take any statins, including Red Yeast Rice.
Get your DHEAs to the top of range in my previous post. You have to figure out the dose by trial and error.
Do not supplement with Estradiol, get another doctor.

Your high SHBG may get better, lower, when you dial in thyroid hormone panel.

100mg progesterone pill, that is likely Prometrium.
Take it at bed time.
Blood was drawn 21 days into cycle, progesterone=1.8, but it should have been around (15 -20)(fifteen- twenty) or more due to high estradiol 222
You likely will need 200mg progesterone at least when you are in high progesterone time in your cycle.

But, you have one more choice that also is more natural.
Check your pregnenolone, likely it is low.
If it is low, supplement with
pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix from Nutricology 150 mg tabs

doing that you would not only raise pregnenolone but also progesterone.

Eventually, you must recall that
pregnenolone
progesterone
cortisol
are on the same metabolic line.

My self (I am a man) I supplement with pregnenolone and my pregnenolone dose is such that my
cortisol(7:30AM)=22

your cortisol=10
you are missing half cortisol.

........
Look up my previous post on how to speed up estrogen metabolization.
also this:
[http://www.fusionhealthstudio.com/estrogen-the-good-the-bad-and-the-ugly/](http://www.fusionhealthstudio.com/estrogen-the-good-the-bad-and-the-ugly/)

reverse T3 16.1

FT3/RT3=15.5 ----------A healthy FT3/RT3 ratio is 20 or greater.

# Thread: leptin reset on light cycle or waking?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-on-light-cycle-or-waking.9423/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### wonder — 2014-01-21T23:04:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-on-light-cycle-or-waking.9423/post-106507>

hi. i have a question: when doing the leptin reset as part of getting hormones and general system / metabolism in order, is it more important to follow light cycles in the morning or with our actual waking time? i ask because as part of a spiritual community, i generally wake up around 4 or 5am, hydrate with water and practice meditation and do energy work/cultivation for a few hours before eating. would it be possible to still wake up this early and not eat the BAB until 8 am since it is in line with the circadian rhythm for digestion, etc? i am aware from ct-7 that ghrelin has a half life etc. and that there are other hormonal firings taking place at waking and soon after...
on such a schedule i would eat dinner around 3 or 4 and sleep around 8 or 9pm with only a very light snack if anything for lunch.
i am not looking to lose weight and my sleep is actually quite good. i am mainly working at healing my system after over 5 years of undetected parasites that wreaked havoc on all organs and systems (they have been removed from body and i am on a prescribed herbal healing regimen. additionally looking to regain 20-30 lbs of lost weight and get a thyroid back into order, and boost general energy. body tends to be cold.

thanks so much for input.

### margor_j — 2014-01-21T23:25:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-on-light-cycle-or-waking.9423/post-106509>

hi there,
wow, sounds like youre in a nice space! something im going to entertain doing again... im relatively new to this forum, and i have seen that question asked more than once. my understand is: you eat within half an hour of waking. this is important to reset ancient pathways that were in use before leptin came along. some mornings (rarely now) if i wake at stupid o'clock (like 4am?!) then yeah, i struggle to eat that early to, dont wanna wake ppl up etc.
iv had some really good guidance on here, and some of the senior members have let me know (in a nice way!) to pull my finger out and do it to the book. so for me, that means eating within half an hour of waking. and if thats really early, then i look at the sun when i can.

hope that helps - im still learning a lot tho, like i said!

namaste
Mags

# Thread: My grounding situation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-01-22T19:18:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/post-106632>

Another day - another discovery for me. We moved my computer to a different room and I used the body volt meter to measure my new set-up. Just like in the other room, without grounding myself, the number was high. When I put my left leg on the grounding mat - the number stayed the same. That surprised me. I switched my legs and then measured it again with my right leg on the mat - the number dropped to its lowest level and stayed that way when I put both legs on the mat. I repeated it several times with the same results. My husband did not experience any of that - his numbers were the same for each leg on the mat. I am not so sure what to do about this newest discovery, but it somehow is connected to all the tingling that I've been feeling in my left leg lately.

### nonchalant — 2014-01-23T01:21:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/post-106653>

Strange, Tanya. The voltage isn't traveling between your foot and hand. Could a pinched nerve do something like that?

### Hope — 2014-01-23T01:24:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/post-106655>

It could be because you are a woman. I can't explain it in the right words. I am sure someone will chime in. BTW I LOVE your sharing your results. I started testing majorly yesterday and have wacky results too. But I trust them. In our house, anything near the walls (many at least) is yucky = yucky wiring.

What's the lowest area in your house? Also I would love to hear your actual numbers, if you have time.

### nonchalant — 2014-01-23T01:36:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/post-106657>

I wonder how your reflexes are in that leg.

### Tanya — 2014-01-23T17:59:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/post-106767>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=106653)
>
> Strange, Tanya. The voltage isn't traveling between your foot and hand. Could a pinched nerve do something like that?

Thank you for your help, nonchalant. Looks like I need to pay more attention to that leg, for sure. Both my legs sustained a lot of damage in my knees and ankles from a previous severe RA attack. My left knee was especially hurt at that time and so even now it can still remind me of that. I also wonder if it is nerve related.... Another possibility could be that I used to give myself B-12 shots into upper part of my left buttock. At times, afterwards it would feel somewhat painful and lumpy. I now stopped injecting them, doing icing there, and using magnet therapy - will see if that will help. I did test my reflexes on that leg and so far they seem to be working. The tingling and even some numbness at times still continues...

### Tanya — 2014-01-23T18:01:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/post-106768>

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=106655)
>
> What's the lowest area in your house? Also I would love to hear your actual numbers, if you have time.

Hope, the lowest number that i measured for myself was in my living room. It was .800 of a volt (not grounded) and 0.005 of a volt (grounded). The highest number for me was in my kitchen, especially near my refrigerators area, we have 2 of them in there - 10,000 volts!!! (not grounded) and .150 of a volt (grounded).

### Hope — 2014-01-23T22:28:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/post-106795>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=106768)
>
> Hope, the lowest number that i measured for myself was in my living room. It was .800 of a volt (not grounded) and 0.005 of a volt (grounded). The highest number for me was in my kitchen, especially near my refrigerators area, we have 2 of them in there - 10,000 volts!!! (not grounded) and .150 of a volt (grounded).

Wow! My BVM doesn't even go to 10K volts. Wow. So when you ground yourself, you take your grounding mat with you? Do you generally see a difference between you and your DH? I am still working on mine to let me test him...

I really wonder too about your leg.

### Tanya — 2014-01-23T22:46:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-grounding-situation.9430/post-106799>

Hope, I have a few mats strategically placed around the house, but I am especially very careful about always using one in the kitchen. Actually, to make sure that I would never not be grounded in there, I have 3 mats placed in my kitchen. I am hoping to eventually convince my husband that we don't need 2 refrigerators...I am not surprised that there is a huge difference in the numbers between him and myself. My non-native EMF environment was extremely huge to compare to what he was exposed.

# Thread: Carpet VOCs....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carpet-vocs.9432/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2014-01-23T01:22:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carpet-vocs.9432/post-106654>

Have any of you dealt with new carpet VOCs? Saw a house that has a lot of great features, low wifi and power lines...and new carpet. Ugh. I have never been in a place with new carpet....but looking at the house today, I got a headache within 20 minutes.

I so need to become a builder and build my own little home. Ugh.

If anyone has input, I would so appreciate it. I know it's a bit off topic.

### freesia — 2014-01-23T01:39:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carpet-vocs.9432/post-106659>

NASA did studies that showed how certain houseplants can help with VOCs. They were looking for ways to purify air in space shuttles.

[http://greenhomeguide.com/askapro/q...plants-to-improve-my-homes-indoor-air-quality](http://greenhomeguide.com/askapro/question/can-i-use-house-plants-to-improve-my-homes-indoor-air-quality)

> Common household products can contain volatile organic compounds (VOCs), gases that pollute indoor air. Paint, paint strippers, cleaning supplies, glues and adhesives all contain VOCs. Breathing these compounds causes symptoms that range from allergic reactions to respiratory tract irritation to more serious conditions like cancer.
>
> There are now paints, carpeting products, and indoor cleaners that have low or no VOCs. Regardless, NASA found that a house plant for every 50 feet will help reduce VOCs and improve home air quality.

I'm not sure whether "reducing" VOCs would be enough in your case or not...either way keep houseplants in mind maybe.

### cantweight — 2014-01-23T02:54:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carpet-vocs.9432/post-106673>

Keeping windows open a tiny bit is supposed to help.

### Hope — 2014-01-23T02:58:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carpet-vocs.9432/post-106674>

Freesia, yes, that is what I wonder. I have an email into my mold MD as well.....we shall see. Thanks so much for that link!

And Christi - I will have the windows WIDE WIDE open if we move into that place...in any weather except high humidity  lol but no kidding. I crave fresh air. always have.

But oh, how I dream of building a green home. I wish there was a local green builder, I'd totally love to work there. Somehow.

It's just so hard to decide...do I jump in there....or keep waiting while living in emf hell and mold.

### caroline — 2014-01-23T09:22:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carpet-vocs.9432/post-106705>

Can you buy a piece of property and then think about building sometime down the track? In the mean time you could camp there!

Someone posted some great plans awhile ago - did you see them?

### Hope — 2014-01-23T23:22:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carpet-vocs.9432/post-106815>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=106705)
>
> Can you buy a piece of property and then think about building sometime down the track? In the mean time you could camp there!
>
> Someone posted some great plans awhile ago - did you see them?

\= my dream, but not sure if we move away from here...so much to consider.

I do have a friend in the mountains I see, and she has property where I could camp. But there are bears, at times and foxes for sure. One rabid one recently got into her house even bc on a warm day she left the door open for her puppies. More than that though, I sleep with a CPAP machine. I feel awful without it....hate sleeping au naturel. But sleeping in nature....would be amazing.

### Eliza — 2014-01-23T10:38:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-with-my-labs.9439/post-106723>

Hi everyone,

I've been on the leptin reset and CT for about 4 weeks now. I've got more history in my journal. I had some labs done a few months before I started the reset, and then just a week ago (about 2 weeks into the reset). Some of them don't seem great and I'd love some advice on what to do next!

I know I haven't done all the recommended labs yet, as I haven't yet told my Dr or endocrinologist (metformin for insulin resistance) yet, as I'm concerned they won't support this approach. I know I will need to find a way to get them to support me, but any help on the labs in the mean time would be wonderful! Thanks in advance

labs before reset:

Vitamin D: 65nM/L (currently on supplementation 10,000iu/day) (ref: 51-140)
Free T4: 14.4pmol/L (ref: 8.2 - 22.0)
TSH: 1.8 mU/L (ref: 0.50 - 4.4)
Free T3: 3.2 pmol/L (ref: 2.0 -7.0)
Testosterone: <0.5nmol/L
17-OH Progesterone: 3.2nmol/L (ref: Follicular <6, Luteal <10)
FSH: 3 IU/L (ref: 2-23 basal, 11-30 midcycle peak, 21-175 post menopause)
LH: 3 IU/L (2-14 basal, 14-110 midcycle, 15-97 post menopause)

### Eliza — 2014-02-07T00:22:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-with-my-labs.9439/post-108767>

Thanks so much for your reply Jansz. I have been doing the reset and ct for about 6 weeks now. Feeling better in a lot of ways but possibly only a little weight loss.

Should I continue until I see further benefits or get retested now? I don't want to get all my labs done if I am still detoxing and they're worse. I'm hopeful that the reset and ct is sufficient to fix my hormones without supplementing. What do you think?

### JanSz — 2014-02-08T00:18:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-with-my-labs.9439/post-108965>

> [Eliza said:](/goto/post?id=108767)
>
> Thanks so much for your reply Jansz. I have been doing the reset and ct for about 6 weeks now. Feeling better in a lot of ways but possibly only a little weight loss.
>
> Should I continue until I see further benefits or get retested now? I don't want to get all my labs done if I am still detoxing and they're worse. I'm hopeful that the reset and ct is sufficient to fix my hormones without supplementing. What do you think?

As far as I know, anything else that you may be doing, will not raise your DHEAs.
Among most commonly analyzed steroid hormones in the blood, DHEAs participation is about 95%, morning cortisol participation is about 4.5%, the remaining 0.5% is for all other.

Your DHEAs is an elephant, take care of it.

Read up here:
[http://www.lef.org/protocols/index.htm](http://www.lef.org/protocols/index.htm)
DHEA Restoration Therapy
[http://www.lef.org/protocols/metabolic\_health/dhea\_restoration\_01.htm](http://www.lef.org/protocols/metabolic_health/dhea_restoration_01.htm)
**Life Extension Suggestions**

****_Optimizing DHEA Blood Levels_****
[http://www.lef.org/protocols/metabolic\_health/dhea\_restoration\_les.htm#AnchorSug](http://www.lef.org/protocols/metabolic_health/dhea_restoration_les.htm#AnchorSug)

Women 275–400 µg/dL (this is blood test)

//////////////////////
You practically do not have any DHEAs.
DHEAS: 4umol/L (ref: 1.7 - 11.5 premenopause, 0.8-6.6 post menopause)

Post your age, and if your status (menstruating or not),
but you want good DHEAs levels regardless of your age.

...

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-with-my-labs.9439/post-110470>

Thanks Jansz. I am 28 and have a normal cycle. I'm looking at a new gp at the moment who will hopefully be able to help me with my DHEA levels.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tv-show-freaks-of-nature-had-wim-hoff-and-a-human-magnet.9443/post-106775>

I'm curious. Has anyone else seen the Weather Channel TV show Freaks of Nature? They featured Wim Hoff (the Ice Man) in a tank of ice for an hour and the episode I saw last night was about human magnets.

The human magnet could stick metal objects on his body and they would stay. The last thing he held with his body was a 178 pound piece of marble. Anybody have any thoughts on this? I don't know what to think but I'm fascinated.

# Thread: Evolution of lactose tolerance likely has nothing to do with calcium needs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-lactose-tolerance-likely-has-nothing-to-do-with-calcium-needs.9451/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-24T15:38:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-lactose-tolerance-likely-has-nothing-to-do-with-calcium-needs.9451/post-106906>

DNA extracted from ancient bones in "Sunny Spain" appears to knock that hypothesis on the head:

> "But here's the thing", says SverrisdÃ³ttir, "if natural selection is driving lactase persistence evolution in a place where people have no problems making vitamin D in their skin, then clearly the vitamin D and calcium explanation (known as the calcium assimilation hypothesis) isn't cutting it. So while the calcium assimilation hypothesis may have some relevance in Northern Europe it's clearly not the whole story".

[http://mbe.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/01/20/molbev.msu049.full.pdf+html](http://mbe.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/01/20/molbev.msu049.full.pdf+html)

# Thread: Doing Leptin Rx, wheres my energy gone? :(
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### margor_j — 2014-01-25T01:56:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106946>

So, Iv changed locations in the past two weeks. i for sure, feel sure this is a way better situation - no neighbours' wifi around, near the ocean etc etc etc.

Im sleeping like iv never known! OMG, if i could id be thanking JK in person with tears streaming down my face!! good lord! iv waited 4 years to be able to sleep!! i literally start yawning around 8pm tho, and as a rule, am asleep by ten. wake anywhere from 5.30-6.30am - sounds all good, but i get up and start my day, usually with a walk on the beach and go to come home and it hits me, just dead exhausted. its only been in the past week so what the hell is it?

i do wonder though, its this my body resetting after all these years of chronic insomnia? is it actually something deeper happening on a reset level? anyone else had this?

i do wonder about the whey protein shakes in the morning,, hmm im off dairy otherwise but whey proteins one of those things you think youre going to be ok, so you buy it to try it.. and where im at now, its a lot easier for me to get up and make a smoothie, it is what it is. id like to think its not that though because thats not a hugely drastic change, its not like iv never had it before or wasnt having it before leaving that city i was in - and im at my parents place, nothing has changed here and i usually feel fine...

thanks

### caroline — 2014-01-25T02:10:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106948>

Hi Margo! Why not make a smoothie using coconut cream and MCT oil and a few eggs?

### margor_j — 2014-01-25T02:15:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106949>

thanks caroline,
im trying to back off eggs 'just incase' im reactive to them? previous tests have shown i am, but i got told that it wasnt a good test (confusion!) or the factor that was tested for reaction, wasnt so reliable..
i talked to tim jackson yesterday, he said i could try pea / rice protein.. i cant get MCT in NZ and have run out of the stuff i buy from america...

hmmm so you think its the whey protein caroline?

### caroline — 2014-01-25T02:33:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106952>

I just know that it doesn't agree with me......don't understand it tho.

How did your consult go?

If you read Ricardo's lipid posts - he talks about adding MCT oil or coconut oil and olive oil to quark cheese for his breakfast and a couple of other things.....

### margor_j — 2014-01-25T02:45:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106955>

yeah, well, iv used it on and off before, and never notice anything drastic, tho TJ said it could be 'unseen' stuff at work.. so who knows. maybe il try breaking up my breakfasts so im not on it every day, and use a pea protein as well.. i do load it with coconut oil. i wondered if it was like a 'healing crisis' given with the monumental leap my sleep pattern has made. the last couple of nites iv gone to bed later, and wake in the morning from the deepest sleep ever..
yeah consult with TJ im still coming to terms with! an hour flies by so fast and i have lots of questions, but i could talk about health stuff all day! at least now, i know more bout digestive issues iv had and how to fix them specifically, and it all helps in building the over all picture im creating.. he went off the extensive testing i had done via a GP last year and also my CDSA.. respect to digestion!

### caroline — 2014-01-25T03:01:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106957>

Did you record your consult?

### margor_j — 2014-01-25T03:17:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106960>

no, i was making notes
never knew there was such a thing as 'progesterone steal' either! thought it was only pregnenolone steal - now im happy i didnt buy pregnenolone a week ago when i was asking!
the GP that helped me overseas really helped me a lot, and hes really good, but theres quite a few things he missed if you compare it with what TJ said about the test.. i have to say, i do feel a diff from these digestive enzymes, and im still on a low dose in comparison with what TJ wants me to go up to, but yeah, its a really nice feeling in the gut!
and so much for raw milk! its so delicious but i stopped drinking it over a week ago - just as well. no dairy again now. well, he said if theres bits in supplements and stuff, thats ok, but dont drink glasses of milk for eg (which i was doing)..
i guess il know more of the nitty gritty after 23andme, but its good to get a handle on some of these basics first

### caroline — 2014-01-25T05:12:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106967>

Did you skype? you can easily recored and re listen....When I try to take notes i miss too much!

### margor_j — 2014-01-25T06:06:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106971>

yeah, what a bugger i didnt record it! but i am pretty pleased with my notes. didnt get a heap of time to ask him questions, but its tweaking that im sure will reap benefits..

# Thread: Prehistoric Shell Mounds Threatened by Rising Sea Level
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prehistoric-shell-mounds-threatened-by-rising-sea-level.9465/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-27T10:30:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prehistoric-shell-mounds-threatened-by-rising-sea-level.9465/post-107029>

This is in Florida. There are huge middens there:

> They’re digging on a huge pile of debris called midden. Lawson says it’s like a prehistoric garbage dump shaped into architectural mounds. The best modern-day analogy for it, he says, is a collection of beer bottles after a college party.
>
> “Having a giant pile of shells outside your house shows just how big of a party you had and how much you gave away and raises your status,” he explains.

They also "believe they’ve found a unique stone Indian burial mound".

[http://news.wfsu.org/post/rising-sea-adds-urgency-south-florida-archeological-digs](http://news.wfsu.org/post/rising-sea-adds-urgency-south-florida-archeological-digs)

See also:

[http://coaps.fsu.edu/nps](http://coaps.fsu.edu/nps)

# Thread: DNA of Mesolithic Hunter
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-27T16:58:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107040>

He would have been darker-skinned than modern Europeans but had blue eyes. They say he would have had difficulty digesting starchy foods. (No suprise there.)

> He was probably lactose intolerant and **had more difficulty digesting starchy foods** than the farmers who transformed diets and lifestyles when they took up tools in the first agricultural revolution. ...
>
> Carbon dating put the remains at around 7,000 years old ...
>
> After several failed attempts, they managed to reconstruct the man's entire genome from DNA found in the root of a third molar. It is the first time researchers have obtained the complete genome of a modern European who lived before the Neolithic revolution. ...
>
> They found that the ancient DNA most closely matched the genetic makeup of people living in northern Europe, in particular Sweden and Finland.

[http://www.theguardian.com/science/2014/jan/26/swarthy-blue-eyed-caveman-dna-tooth](http://www.theguardian.com/science/2014/jan/26/swarthy-blue-eyed-caveman-dna-tooth)

Here's the paper:

[http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature12960.html](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature12960.html)

### nonchalant — 2014-01-27T18:56:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107047>

> But scientists may have over-estimated the impact farming had in shaping the human immune system, because tests on the hunter-gatherer's DNA found that he already carried mutations that boost the immune system to tackle various nasty bugs. Some live on in modern Europeans today.

Yeah, Dr. K could have told them that... Immune system enhancements came first, then the enlarged brain.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-28T10:44:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107130>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=107040)
>
> They say he would have had difficulty digesting starchy foods. (No suprise there.)

I haven't got a Nature subscription, so can't see the full article. But thinking about this one, I wonder if this is more a matter of speculation than a finding. AFAIK, what you can digest is more down to your gut microbiome than your DNA. I think there can, though, be some interesting interactions here. Perhaps they mean he's got that human leukocyte antigen allele that's relatively common in Finland and doesn't go well with a starchy diet ... at any rate, if you've got an overgrowth of the wrong bacteria in your gut.

But maybe it was just a generalised remark about Hunter-gatherers -- we would not _expect_ them to eat this or that. In which case it's not a finding; it's speculation.

### nonchalant — 2014-01-28T16:54:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107163>

They always picture the ancients as having scraggly hair. But I have seen pictures of some aborigines with braided or otherwise cared-for hair.
Hmmm, this guy could look like one of the Bee Gees with a little TLC...

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-29T12:05:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107284>

Another report (from the University of Queensland) on this makes sense of that throwaway comment about starch in the _Grauniad_:

> “This Mesolithic hunter-gatherer carried the gene for lactose-intolerance consistent with an inability to digest dairy products, and also **saliva amylase genes indicative of a low-starch diet**,” Dr Sturm said.

[http://www.uq.edu.au/news/article/2...eals-origins-of-blue-eyes-lactose-intolerance](http://www.uq.edu.au/news/article/2014/01/mesolithic-man-reveals-origins-of-blue-eyes-lactose-intolerance)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### healthman — 2014-01-28T11:39:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107132>

I was wondering how many times a week is recommended for a healthly person to have bone broth or people who are not so healthy how many times should they have broths?

### caroline — 2014-01-28T12:19:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107135>

I think the more the better .... a cup a day at least would be a good warm drink .... way better for us than coffee.

### Lahelada — 2014-01-28T12:26:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107136>

Looking at my coffee right now. Sorry no contest. I will have to do both  But as far as good for you you are absolutely right. I second what Caroline said a cup a day should be the minimum.

### Andrea — 2014-01-28T13:14:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107141>

Does the timing of bone broth consumption matter? I.e. would it be more beneficial to drink it in the evening than in the morning or vice versa?

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-28T13:31:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107143>

> [Rerun said:](/goto/post?id=107141)
>
> Does the timing of bone broth consumption matter? I.e. would it be more beneficial to drink it in the evening than in the morning or vice versa?

I should drink it when you eat meat, since it helps protein digestion.

Weston Price used to supervise special dinners for orphans and part of the meal would be good quality meat that he'd have the cooks grill (broil) rare. The meat would then be cut up and served in broth to try to make sure its food value was assimilated. Though he's dated in some things, it's amazing how much thought that man put into diet all those years ago. Nowadays the kids would have food laden with carbs and industrial seed oils thrown at them, and the contract would go to the cheapest suppliers and caterers.

### nicld — 2014-01-28T16:20:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107152>

I use it with thinly shaved raw beef or venison with some raw leeks and mushrooms. The hot broth slightly cooks the meat and is oh so yummy. I still have to make a great big pot this weekend and freeze a bunch.

### healthman — 2014-01-28T19:39:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107190>

Thanks for the answers everyone
The main reason I asked was because I thought I read and I may be wrong on this that one of Jacks blogs mentioned only using bone broths liberally!
So wanted to double check.

Thanks

### sooperb — 2014-01-28T19:45:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107191>

In place of coffee? With the butter and coconut oil

On a completely different subject, I made some coconut manna in my Vitamix today just to see if I could. It's sitting there looking delicious and it is quite sweet. Looking up coconut meat online it suggests that there is about 6 gs of sugar in it. Now I don't know whether to eat it or dump it. Thoughts please.

### cinnamon — 2014-01-28T21:27:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107203>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=107191)
>
> ...I made some coconut manna in my Vitamix today just to see if I could. It's sitting there looking delicious and it is quite sweet. Looking up coconut meat online it suggests that there is about 6 gs of sugar in it. Now I don't know whether to eat it or dump it. Thoughts please.

Is it just pure coconut? I buy something called "creamed coconut" that sounds like it would be the same thing. The nutrition label says it has 2g of carbs per tablespoon.

It is good melted with 100% chocolate to which you add flaked coconut, chopped nuts and cocoa nibs. Yummy!

_To make about six of these decadent treats:_
I put about a tsp of coconut oil in the pot to start the melting process. Use very low heat - the idea is to melt it, not to cook it. Add about 40g of chocolate and 3 tbsp of the creamed coconut. You can sweeten a little if you find the 100% chocolate too bitter. I put about a quarter teaspoon of xylitol and a drop of pure vanilla extract, but I suppose you could use a drop or two of stevia. After it melts, take it off the heat and allow to cool slightly before you throw in a handful of each of chopped raw nuts and cocoa nibs and about a half a cup of unsweetened flaked coconut. Drop by spoonful onto parchment paper, sprinkle with a little more flaked coconut and let set in the fridge.

WARNING - these can be addictive. It isn't necessarily a good idea to make a larger batch! LOL

### BJK77 — 2014-01-28T21:39:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107208>

How long do you cook your bone broth for? Is just a few hours enough or do we have to do 24 hours like the Weston Price foundation recommends?

### caroline — 2014-01-28T22:06:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107216>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=107136)
>
> Looking at my coffee right now. Sorry no contest. I will have to do both  But as far as good for you you are absolutely right. I second what Caroline said a cup a day should be the minimum.

yep - I do both ... can't seem to do without my BPC at the moment. I love what Nicole does with her bone broth!

### sooperb — 2014-01-29T14:46:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107301>

I have already thought of half a dozen ways to use it along those lines but obviously I don't want to spoil my eating plan

Bone broth cooked for 24 hours? There'd surely be nothing left, it would all evaporate unless you started off with a vat full? I give mine several hours but not even all day.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-29T15:17:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107302>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=107301)
>
> Bone broth cooked for 24 hours? There'd surely be nothing left, it would all evaporate unless you started off with a vat full? I give mine several hours but not even all day.

Some people talk of "24-hour bone broth". I don't know that the WAPF necessarily recommends that. Chefs seem to say around 2 hours (or even less) for fish stock and a few hours longer for meat bones.

This from Sally Fallon at the WAPF has recipes that talk of 6-8 hours for chicken and 12 or more for beef:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful](http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful)

I believe you can get unpleasant flavours if you over-cook bones -- either with too long a time or too high a temperature or both. I have read something about the chemistry of what happens but don't recall. AFAIK, as with stews, it should really be low enough to _only just_ simmer.

Anyone who's interested, I know Sean Croxton's got a video of a chef demonstrating -- I think this must be it:

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JCLDGsw7BOw&feature=youtu.be](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JCLDGsw7BOw&feature=youtu.be)

### Andrea — 2014-01-29T15:49:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107304>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=107301)
>
> Bone broth cooked for 24 hours? There'd surely be nothing left, it would all evaporate unless you started off with a vat full? I give mine several hours but not even all day.

I make my bone broth in a pressure cooker...
I keep the safety valve open though, so there is only atmospheric pressure inside. The stove is on the lowest heat and the water in the pot is just about simmering. The lid is tight enough to prevent evaporation, other than the small amounts that leave through the valve. So I can have a batch on the stove for 24 hours - or more - with minimal water loss.

(And no, I don't kill food in my pressure cooker anymore, this is the only thing I use it for nowadays.)

### nonchalant — 2014-01-29T16:58:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107311>

I cook my bone broth gently over a series of days in a crock pot, cooling it at night. But I'll take samples from it daily. 2-3 days is average. The bones really come clean after a couple days.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-29T20:37:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107333>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=107302)
>
> Some people talk of "24-hour bone broth". I don't know that the WAPF necessarily recommends that. Chefs seem to say around 2 hours (or even less) for fish stock and a few hours longer for meat bones.
>
> This from Sally Fallon at the WAPF has recipes that talk of 6-8 hours for chicken and 12 or more for beef:
>
> [http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful](http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful)
>
> I believe you can get unpleasant flavours if you over-cook bones -- either with too long a time or too high a temperature or both. I have read something about the chemistry of what happens but don't recall. AFAIK, as with stews, it should really be low enough to _only just_ simmer.

I just got home and looked up in my copy of _Understanding Cooking_ by Cesarani, Lundberg, and Kotschevar which I got off a relative who was a Chem. Eng. academic and who, as an extra, used to teach the chemistry of food to catering students.

Cesarani, Lundberg, and Kotschevar is very hot on technique and very science-informed. They say:

> To make 5 litres of good stock, use two kilogrammes of bones and meat, 6 litres of water and about Â½ kilogramme of chopped vegetables … mirepoix … \[and a\] bouquet garni.
>
> … the mirepoix is better added after the bones and meat have been cooked for about half the cooking period; otherwise the vegetables may give a slightly bitter flavour to the stock because of overcooking.
>
> Meat may be removed when tender and the bones left in to continue to cook. The meat is used for other dishes …
>
> Depending on the size of the bones and the way they're cut, a stock from meat and bones is simmered from 4 to 10 hours; from poultry 2 to 6 hours; and from fish 20 minutes. …
>
> If stock has extracted a proper amount of gelatine, it will gel when cold. …
>
> The best bones to use are those from young animals. These contain more gelatine and the marrow contains more flavouring ingredients. Shin and knuckle bones are best, with neck bones next. The bones are best cut into 4 inch lengths and split.

They add that you can reduce the stock after you've made it: this is a glaze.

So, yeah, while Sally is not saying 24 hours, the WAPF cooking times are over-long and out of line both with the practice of chefs and with what the food science people say to do. In particular, it seems fish bones shouldn't be simmered for very long at all. IIRC, the stock will taste pretty bad if you do.

Chefs nowadays also make a point of saying to cool the stock rapidly after you've made it. I don't think anyone bothered much in the old days. But, then, in the old days no-one in the catering trade had much of a clue about health -- chefs used to die very young from the fumes from solid-fuel burning stoves building up in unventilated basement kitchens, helped along by vast quantities of alcohol drunk to slake their thirst. It took Escoffier to change all that:

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/food/0/20123168r](http://www.bbc.co.uk/food/0/20123168r)

Obviously a warm gelatine solution is a pretty good breeding ground for goodness'-knows-what.

IIRC, Chef Lance in the Underground Wellness video links earlier shows how to cool a stock-pot rapidly.

### cinnamon — 2014-01-29T22:51:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107346>

For beef broth, I will get it started on the stove and then move to the oven. I leave it in the same pot - a big stock pot with a lid. I leave the lid on and set the oven temperature for 200 degrees F or just slightly under. This should keep the stock simmering and not boiling. Last time I left it for about 30 hours. It was excellent. The only part of leaving it so long that I didn't like, was that it smells good all night long when I want to be sleeping and not thinking about food.

It was my understanding that it is best to just have a few bubbles coming to the surface and that the stock should NOT be boiling if you want good collagen levels. I'm not sure if that's just a conventional wisdom belief or if there might actually be some science behind it.

### Lahelada — 2014-02-07T18:19:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-108902>

Just making my bone broth. I am roasting the bones before using them but that is probably ,more of an epicurean instead of epigenetic thing  .A chef told me that the the broth tastes better. I add all the good herbs ,parsley for boron,onions for quercetin, turmeric for detox . I have not yet decided for how long I will cook it, but very probably 6 hours with a a bit of vinegar. Half an hour before the end of cooking time I add vegetables that have been roasted in the pan where the bones were roasting in.

### Gracelind — 2014-02-07T22:21:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-108943>

Remember, as the water evaporates, you can always add more while simmering. Or you can refrigerate or freeze the concentrated broth and add water at serving. Or use the concentrated broth in another dish.

Some people keep the pot on the stove or the crockpot brewing, adding more water all week long to the same bones. The advantage to a long simmer would be leaching more of the minerals out of the bones. But it could be detrimental to the gelatin and raise glutamine (natural MSG) levels in the broth--good or bad depending on your personal context.

# Thread: How not to do research
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-not-to-do-research.9474/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-01-28T16:28:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-not-to-do-research.9474/post-107154>

Not sure where to post this link but this article, promoting a TV programme epitomises how misguided some "research" can be:
[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/a...life.html?ito=email-healthnewsletter-20140128](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2546975/One-twin-gave-sugar-gave-fat-Their-experiment-change-YOUR-life.html?ito=email-healthnewsletter-20140128)

### nonchalant — 2014-01-28T16:39:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-not-to-do-research.9474/post-107156>

I think that the object of the typical "research" is to be able to write up a conclusion that would sound reasonable to someone with a smattering of science in their educational background. I'm not really sure why they don't just make everything up, it would be easier and fewer rats would have to die.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-28T21:52:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-not-to-do-research.9474/post-107212>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=107154)
>
> Not sure where to post this link but this article, promoting a TV programme epitomises how misguided some "research" can be:
> [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/a...life.html?ito=email-healthnewsletter-20140128](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2546975/One-twin-gave-sugar-gave-fat-Their-experiment-change-YOUR-life.html?ito=email-healthnewsletter-20140128)

What I found disappointing was that he was repeating misconceptions that a modicum of research online in places like pubmed could have cleared up. Or if you're supposed to be looking at low carb diets, how about a phoning round people like Steve Phinney till you get pointed in the right direction? They're making a TV programme for goodness' sake: it's not as if people who know about these things won't talk to them.

He was also extrapolating in a hurry from his own (brief and probably badly set-up) experience of a ketogenic diet. Does he suppose whole civilisations have been troubled by poor mental performance, lackadaisical physical performance, and constipation?

This is interesting:

> One of the words you hear a lot when people talk about very low-carb diets is ketosis. This is where your body makes chemicals called ketones, which can act as fuel for the brain, which can't use fat. But they're not great brain food. While I wasn't distracted by hunger for the month, I felt thick-headed, and this was most evident in a stock trading competition with Chris.

He knows enough to have heard about ketones. He doesn't know enough to know you have to adapt to them.

I think he needs to know a lot more than he does know. He's also not aware of information from many different sources. He needs to become aware of that and to synthesise the information. So he should ask himself:

1\. If ketosis is not a normal healthful metabolic state, why do babies spend so much time in it?

2\. How have cultures like the Eskimo and the Plains Indians managed on diets with very little, at times no, carbohydrate in?

3\. How did the Fur Trade -- the equivalent of a billion dollar multinational business in the 18th and 19th centuries -- manage to run with the _voyageurs_ living off pemmican while carrying out incredibly arduous physical tasks?

4\. Why have sports teams -- for example the Australian Cricket Team -- switched to a low-carb diet, if it's so bad for physical performance:

[http://www.thelivinlowcarbshow.com/...kner-2013-low-carb-down-under-seminar-series/](http://www.thelivinlowcarbshow.com/shownotes/9196/759-dr-peter-brukner-2013-low-carb-down-under-seminar-series/)

(There's at least one Australian Rules Football club doing the same; and I'll bet you there are football teams in England, both Rugby Football ("rugger") and Association Football ("soccer"), teams either already doing this or looking into it.)

5\. Why is there a tradition going back thousands of years of the practice of fasting in various religions, since that gets you into ketosis, if not because it helps with calmness and mental acuity:

> Howbeit this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting.

[http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+17:21&version=KJV](http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+17%3A21&version=KJV)

(Going a bit off the point, but while I'm a religious sceptic, I'm also too sceptical to dismiss that kind of thing. I actually know two different people, an Anglican priest (before he was ordained) and a nurse, who both described something similar. In each case they were dealing with an very disturbed and angry person, and each said they "felt" something come out of the person and "pass through" them. In the first case the man sort of slumped afterwards; in the second, the nurse said the patient then became tractable. Neither had attempted to _do_ anything; these incidents just happened. Maybe these were demons -- or call it a kind energy or whatever you want -- but _something_ unusual was obviously going on.

And maybe someone functioning really well at a mental level and with the biological basis for a calm, steady energy from fasting could be more aware of dimensions of existence that pass most of us by.)

6\. Where would people in northern Europe, where he's living, be getting carbohydrate from all year round before the advent of farming -- before, indeed, modern container shipping? No mesolithic hunter could eat gigantic strawberries and over-bred super-sweet peaches at any time of the year let alone during February in England.

To sum up: there's a lot of questions he's not asking himself, and a lot of information he's not become aware of and not tried to synthesise. What he's saying just doesn't make sense in the wider picture. Therefore, he should realise that he can't be right.

# Thread: Seaweed

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107180>

I try to eat as much seaweed as possible, but I seldom use it in its rehydrated form..with the exception of putting it in my bone broth. Most of the time I add it to my already prepared dishes or just eat it straight out of the bag. I wonder now if I am still getting all the benefits from doing so...

### sooperb — 2014-01-28T19:18:22+0200

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107322>

sooperb, as much as I like the crunchy wakame, from now on I am going to try to use much more of it being pre-soaked. I tried to do it briefly this morning, just for like 15 min. and I was astonished just how much water it had absorbed. So, I can only imagine what a dehydrating effect it was doing to me in that dry form. I did not like the taste of the water from it, though and so, I just added it to my broth.....

### caroline — 2014-01-30T03:04:56+0200

### Inger — 2014-01-30T10:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107425>

Jack does have said fresh seaweed is best... then comes dehydrated.

So I figure when you soak the raw dehydrated seaweed it will be a bit closer? I mostly make seaweed water and drink it and then puree the seaweed after a day or two with some more water and drink it too, it is nice, like a green smoothie.

I do use Dulce in my raw fish meals, I cut it with a scissor in small slices and mix it with the raw fish, I love that. But Dulce is not good for making soaked seaweed me thinks. The best for that are Wakame, Kombu, Sea Spaghetti. The Dulce do not really get soft. So I use different seaweeds a little different way.

I never cook my seaweed, only if I add it to broth. Otherwise I see no idea in cooking them, really. I think it might make them less healthy. That is why I also buy raw dried seaweeds, not roasted or dried with high heat. I really can taste a difference with different quality of seaweeds. The french, raw dried seaweeds are by far the best I have had so far.

That seaweed water made from high quality, raw dried seaweeds is addictive, crazy yummy!

### Tanya — 2014-01-31T18:10:29+0200

It made me wonder why - since I was doing everything else correctly. So, the last few days after my small experiment has proven to me where was my mistake.

### sooperb — 2014-01-31T18:16:10+0200

### Josh — 2014-01-31T20:25:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107609>

I figured out that the only way I would reliably consume it was if I blended it. When I steam vegetables or meats, I put it in the water or around the meat or vegetables and then blend it with the water at the end and drink as broth. I also put it in my fermented vegetables such as kimchi and sauerkraut and then blend them with some water as a drink as well. It all does a lot smoother and brings out interesting tastes. I guess you could soak and blend any type raw as well. Probably will try that too.

### Tanya — 2014-02-12T17:48:33+0200

I need to slow down, really slow down with my sea weed consumption…...[http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2013/08/21/superfood-seaweed-health-benefits\_n\_3786076.html](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2013/08/21/superfood-seaweed-health-benefits_n_3786076.html)

### Josh — 2014-02-12T18:34:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-109798>

There is enough iodine in some varieties to give my wife and I iodine excess symptoms. Typically we need more selenium to handle the higher iodine content seaweeds. As the article notes, it varies a bit from variety to variety and I have seen this in my own usage. Dr. Kharrazian emphasizes how people with Hashimoto's are better off never having a larger than trace dose of iodine as it may trigger AI issues. It has been discussed elsewhere on the forum. Many people are very careful with "supplements" and ignore similar issues with "food".

### Tanya — 2014-02-12T18:59:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-109803>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=109798)
>
> There is enough iodine in some varieties to give my wife and I iodine excess symptoms. Typically we need more selenium to handle the higher iodine content seaweeds. As the article notes, it varies a bit from variety to variety and I have seen this in my own usage. Dr. Kharrazian emphasizes how people with Hashimoto's are better off never having a larger than trace dose of iodine as it may trigger AI issues. It has been discussed elsewhere on the forum. Many people are very careful with "supplements" and ignore similar issues with "food".

I completely agree with what you are saying, Josh. I assumed that I was not lacking in selenium since I was already supplementing it and eating brazil nuts in addition to that. But I went so overboard on seaweed, and that was on top of 2 seafood meals a day, that I was having. I just kept ignoring the fact that my temperature kept getting lower and lower. That was not very smart of me... I checked a wakame package this morning and surprise, surprise - iodine was not even listed in there.

### Ken23 — 2014-05-30T17:55:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-128934>

Tanya, I'm noticing a similar reaction when I up my seaweed. Hands and feet become much colder. Not sure if this means I have Hashi. Did you cut back on the seaweed or up your selenium?

I am still experimenting with my thyroid situation, Ken, but I most definitely cut back on the amount of seaweed I was having before and I also went off my selenium supplement altogether as I felt that I was getting enough of it in my food.

Ken, I don’t know how much of seaweed you are having, but I believe that it is so easy to overdose on it and you can harm your thyroid that way.

### Ken23 — 2014-05-31T19:17:24+0300

### nicld — 2014-01-28T18:55:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-testing.9479/post-107181>

Ok, I got my 23andme results back and ran them through the MTHFRSupport app and if anyone has any insight into what they mean I would greatly appreciate it. I will be trying for a consult with Dr Tim to decipher them a little more maybe next month as will as doing more research on the results. These are all the ones below are ones that showed up red.

**DETOX**
CYP1B1 L432V
NAT1 A803G
SOD2
SOD2 A16V
CTH S4031I
RARA

**Allergy Mold**
No red but both HLA yellow

**IgE**
No reds

**IgG**
FCGR2A
IRF5

**CLOTTING FACTORS**
CEPT
ITGB3 T196C

**METHYLATION**
BHMT-08
COMT H62H
COMT V158M
MAO A R297R
MTHFD1 C105T
MTHFR C677T
NOS2 (BOTH)

**MITOCHONDRIAL FUNCTION**
ONLY 3 YELLOWS, REST GREEN

**OTHER IMMUNE FACTORS**
ATG16L1 (I think this one is for Chrohns)

**SULFONOTRANSFERASE**
SULT1A1

I will work on getting a summary of the stuff that 23andme provided but I do remember that I am 99.7% of European ancestry (duh).

It also said that I did not have a genetic risk of celiac but I do have it. Which makes one wonder how much more power environment has over genetics (huge I think).

Thanks

Nicole

### nonchalant — 2014-01-28T19:46:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-testing.9479/post-107192>

The MTHFR support app gives a spreadsheet. You can click on one column in that spreadsheet to open a browser window to some relevant research on SNPedia.

Yes, my problem with 23andme is that they don't seem to realize that genes can be activated or deactivated based on your environment.

# Thread: Amazing glass bottles that help restructure water..wow
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-glass-bottles-that-help-restructure-water-wow.9480/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2014-01-28T19:00:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-glass-bottles-that-help-restructure-water-wow.9480/post-107182>

Just discovered this amazing source for water storage....wow. here is what what they do accg to Dr L Williams on her blog post about water....

These violet bottles _**filter out all degrading light frequencies, allowing only healthy UV(a) and far infrared waves inside that help restructure the deformed microclusters in water that have been damaged from turbulent plumbing systems, light, and chemical**_s. Used in ancient Egypt for preserving food, this amazing biophotonic glass makes even your filtered water more vital and â€œwetter,â€￾ providing **proper intercellular hydration** and therefore translating into less of a need to consume excessive water. In fact, the 500 ml Violiv water bottle is the equivalent of a little more than 2 8-ounce glasses of water â€“ just enough to quench your thirst while youâ€™re out and about.

~ that's like exactly what Dr K just talked about in the webinar and here. Biophotonic glass. Wow.

### Josh — 2014-01-28T19:27:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-glass-bottles-that-help-restructure-water-wow.9480/post-107188>

### Josh — 2014-01-28T20:10:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-glass-bottles-that-help-restructure-water-wow.9480/post-107195>

Functionality

The sun emits visible and invisible light parts. The visible spectrum becomes discernable to our eyes when white light is channeled through a glass prism. At the same time though, light also contains a part which is invisible to our eyes and lies in the radius of electromagnetic wavelengths of infra red and UV-A.

Sunlight enables all plants to grow. If they continue to be exposed to the sun after reaching maturity, the effect of the light changes and accelerates the molecular decaying process. Miron violet glass works like a natural filter that only lets the sunlight that protects and improves the quality of premium and sensitive substances.

There is a large spectrum of packaging materials available nowadays, of which many are permeable to visible light. The following graphics show to which degree light can penetrate the different materials: (go to link below)

Miron Violet glass blocks the complete spectrum of visible light with the exception of the violet part.
At the same time it allows a certain part to be permeable for radiation in the spectral range of UV-A, and infra red light. This unique combination offers optimal protection against the ageing processes that are released by visible light, thus lengthening durability and potency of products.

In order to substantiate these advantages, Miron Violet Glass AG has conducted a series of tests themselves and in cooperation with scientific institutions.
Further confirmation of functionality comes from the science of biophotonics

[http://www.miron-glas.com/en/Functionality](http://www.miron-glas.com/en/Functionality)
