# Thread: New labs vs 2 yrs ago results
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-vs-2-yrs-ago-results.9590/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sandra — 2014-02-04T02:28:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-vs-2-yrs-ago-results.9590/post-108220>

I have some recent results and some from a few years ago. Most things are 'within range', but some have changed A LOT in 2 years. I wonder if that has any meaning, even though they say the results are still within range (except for cholesterol).

I am especially wondering why TSH might increase, and why the iron levels are back in line from a few years ago, but I'm still tired??

The main things that have changed are ...

Test ................... Nov 2011 compared to Jan 2013

UREA...................4.7 .........................5.3.....mmol/L
Creatinine ...........66 ...........................52 ......umol/L
BUN/Creatinine....17.674.......................25.297 ............the only significant changes that I can think of over this time have been going Paleo and working in an office 3 days a week starting 12 months ago and drinking RO water and magnetico for the last 3 months

T Bilirubin...........8..............................16........umol/L
GGT...................7..............................22.......U/L
Cholesterol.........5.3............................6.3 ....mmol/L
HDL..................2.1..........................2.37.....mmol/L
LDL...................2.6........................3.56 .....mmol/L \*\*\*\*this looks really high
Triglyceride........1.3............................0.7 ......mmol/L

Big changes in iron
iron...................19 ...........................24........umol/L
TIBC..................84............................68........umol/L
Trans Sat...........23............................35........%
Ferritin..............20............................41........Ug/L

Thyroid
TSH..................1.6..........................2..........mU/L

Platelets.............206.........................247

The rest of the results are ...

BLOOD COUNT

Haemoglobin........................ 141 g/L
Haematocrit......................... 0.42
RCC Red Cell Count............... 4.5 10\*12/L
MCV Mean Cel Volume.......... 94 fL
Meal Cell haemoglobin.......... 32 pg
WCC ................................ 4.8 10\*9/L
Neutrophils .................... 2.2 10\*9/L
Lymphocytes....................... 2 10\*9/L
Monocytes.......................... 0.5 10\*9/L
Eosinophils.......................... 0.1 10\*9/L
Basophils............................... 0.05 10\*9/L

Platelets................................ 247 10\*9/L

CHOLESTEROL

Chol...................................... 6.3 mmol/L
Trigs .......................................0.07 mmol/L
HDL ....................................... 2.37 mmol/L
LDL........................................ 3.56 mmol/L
Tot Chol/HDL

THYROID FUNTION

TSH............................. 2 mU/L

ANAEMIA

Iron................ 24 umol/L
TIBC............. 68 umol/L
Trans Sat........ 35 %
Ferritin.......... 41 ug/L

ROUTINE CHEMISTRY

Sodium................ 140 mmol/L
Potassium................ 4.3 mmol/L
Chloride................ 103 mmol/L
Bicarbonate................ 26 mmol/L
Anion Gap ................15 mmol/L
Ca (Corr)................ 2.47 mmol/L
Phosphate................ 1.2 mmol/L
Urea................ 5.3 mmol/L
Urate ................0.34 mmol/L
Creatinine ................52 mmol/L
eGFR ................over 90 mmol/L
Fast Glucose................ 4.4 mmol/L
Total Protein................ 72 g/L
Albumin................ 38 g/L
Globulin................ 34 g/L
T Bilirubin ................16 umol/L
ALP Alk Phos................ 43 U/L
AST ................22 U/L
ALT ................21 U/L
GGT Gamma GT ................22 U/L
LDH ................156 U/L
Cholesterol ................6.3 mmol/L
Triglyceride................ 0.7 mmol/L
Iron ................24 umol/L
CRP C Reactive Protein................ <5 mg/L
Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate................ 5 mm/hr

I don't know the significance of what would make these readings change so much and whether there's anything to be concerned about, or if there are any clues that might lead to something else.

If anyone can see anything that jumps out at them, I'd love to hear it.

Hoping to get some other results soon.

Thanks

you're dehydrated - most likely due to your change in working environment. LDL........................................ 3.56 mmol/L= 137.66
HDL ....................................... 2.37 mmol/L= 91.647

Total cholesterol is somewhat high.
If you have a low DHEAs and raise it, your cholesterol should look better.
On day 2-4 of your menstrual cycle do blood test, check
pregnenolone, progesterone, estradiol, estrone, estriol, total estrogens, DHEAs
post results on this thread. Look up my posts on this thread.
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/#post81508](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/#post81508)

# Thread: Forum UPGRADE: we're now running on XenForo
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Support — 2014-02-04T08:40:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108270>

Hi all,

As of February 4, 2013 we've installed a new forum software called XenForo to take the place of vBulletin. We've done our best to import ALL content from the old forum into this new platform, but we will be relying on you, the forum community, to report any bugs you encounter. Please be patient as we continue optimize this forum over the next several days.

You should be able to log in with your usual username and password, and use this forum exactly as you would the old one.

Feel free to post in this thread with any questions, comments or concerns.

### fitness@home — 2014-02-04T12:53:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108274>

It looks as though everyone should be able to add an avatar themselves now. Like the new "look" of the forum. Will poke around today and see if I notice any bugs.

### nicld — 2014-02-04T16:12:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108281>

This looks neat. Might take a little while to get used to but change it good

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-04T16:38:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108282>

Thumbs up from me...I like the type face a lot better, highlights etc....and finally fitness and a few others will be joined by some avatars.

### nonchalant — 2014-02-04T16:54:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108285>

Hey, I got my old avatar back! Guess it was hanging around on the server drive somewhere.

### Josh — 2014-02-04T20:21:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108310>

It is prettier and faster and a little more fun. I am not so thrilled about the "trophy" feature...

### tellmisty — 2014-02-04T21:47:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108327>

Yeah, those trophies are annoying.

The reason we switched to XenForo from vBulletin is because it's built using responsive design. This means people should be able to access the forums pretty easily on mobile devices.  A member of our community, Paul, told me about them and while I was resistant to changing anything ('cause y'all can lose your minds!), I looked into it and it actually seemed like it would cause minimal waves with a HUGE upside in the responsive design department. Thanks, Paul!!!

### nicld — 2014-02-04T22:09:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108332>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=108310)
>
> It is prettier and faster and a little more fun. I am not so thrilled about the "trophy" feature...

I agree about the trophies. If I want trophies I would be on Sparkpeople more

### tellmisty — 2014-02-04T22:51:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-upgrade-were-now-running-on-xenforo.9591/post-108345>

I think we figured out how to delete the trophies. ;-) And ... we can like posts and you can see who's liked your post! That's kinda cool.

### caroline — 2014-02-04T22:56:33+0200

# Thread: As Predicted...Cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/as-predicted-cancer.9594/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-02-04T18:45:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/as-predicted-cancer.9594/post-108298>

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-26014693](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-26014693)

Having lived in India and China, I would say everything from AIDS to Cancer is under diagnosed by at least 50%. I met a young researcher in a cafe in Delhi in 2005 who had quit laboratory research to work on AIDS. When I asked him if there was any hope in India, he shook his head....

I wonder if the increase in the developing world is the blessings of our "modern life" or just better diagnosis?

I wondered what the "Delayed Parenthood..." meant and found this answer on Reddit:

"Decreased parity reduces the risk of some gynecologic cancers because there are fewer cycles of hormonal activation/deactivation. However, the relationship is more complex than that as the dose-response is very nonlinear. For example, a single year of oral contraceptive use decreases risk of ovarian cancer by around 10%, and 5 yrs decreases the risk by around 30-50%, which is disproportionately large relative to the number of ovulatory cycles avoided."

# Thread: So there's a unicode character for that, too
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-theres-a-unicode-character-for-that-too.9601/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-04T22:25:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-theres-a-unicode-character-for-that-too.9601/post-108336>

I was wondering what Rx was. Seems it means a medical prescription. Turns out there's a unicode character for it, too -- an R with a little slash across. ℞

[http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/211E/index.htm](http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/211E/index.htm)

On a Mac open up "Character Viewer" and search under "prescription".

# Thread: Some dirt could be good for us
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-dirt-could-be-good-for-us.9602/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-02-04T22:32:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-dirt-could-be-good-for-us.9602/post-108339>

Interesting article explaining how eating dirt is beneficial.. [http://www.yesmagazine.org/issues/how-to-eat-like-our-lives-depend-on-it/how-dirt-heals-us](http://www.yesmagazine.org/issues/how-to-eat-like-our-lives-depend-on-it/how-dirt-heals-us)

Jack was telling us the same in his recent cooking video.

### Josh — 2014-02-09T03:33:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-dirt-could-be-good-for-us.9602/post-109196>

The soil foodweb mirrors our own micrbiome. The really interesting thing that Jack brought into focus is that our micrbiome actually senses our environment and actively modulates our response to it. We have coevolved with our bugs and they are invested in keeping us in homeostasis so they have a home, food, transport, etc.. I have been focused on this as I am 10 months into my main biohack of the year which is hookworm innoculation. Removing helminths from our microbiome is akin to removing large predators from an ecosystem, it affects the behavior of many other species. It is an entire trophic level. I am guessing that the helminths have multiple ways that they sense our environment, both locally in the gut and the external environment; and then they have mutliple ways to modulate our immune response and the behavior of other microorganisms within the gut. Today I learned that worms even fight with each other to keep their territory inside us. I now understand that a "parasite" is only a parasite if the system is thrown out of balance by it. Consider how many ungerminated seeds lie in the soil because the conditions do not support their growth. I see this as how our guts can function with the right mix of organisms adapted to our local environment and diet. Note "local" environment, because as I see it taking our body and bugs for a long fast rip is akin to throwing my garden in the back of my truck and driving or flying it somewhere else. Inevitably, some plants may thrive while others may die. Imagine the signals they may send to each other and the environment when things change so quickly. I doubt our bugs have evolved fast enough to handle the mismatches of our new lives and they and we suffer together.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Journal of a Trapper by Osborne Russell
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-of-a-trapper-by-osborne-russell.9603/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-04T22:57:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-of-a-trapper-by-osborne-russell.9603/post-108349>

Here it is:

[http://user.xmission.com/~drudy/mtman/html/ruslintr.html](http://user.xmission.com/~drudy/mtman/html/ruslintr.html)

There are a few things of interest from the Paleo point of view buried in there. For example:

> we discovered a large Grizzly Bear about 200 yards ahead of us: one of our hunters approached and shot him dead on the spot. We all rode up and dismounted to butcher him: he was an enormous animal a hideous brute a savage looking beast. On removing the skin we found the fat on his back measured six inches deep. ...

> Here we encamped on a small clear spot and killed the fattest Elk I ever saw. It was a large Buck the fat on his rump measured seven inches thick ...

> The squaws were employed cutting and drying meat for two days at the end of which the ground on which the village stood seemed covered with meat scaffolds bending beneath their rich loads of fat Buffaloe meat ...

So much for the "wild game is lean" delusion, engaged in many even within Paleo, including Professor Cordain.

Interestingly, we also have this:

> Allen made an observation which I shall never forget Said he I have been watching these cows some time and I can see but one that is poor enough to Kill" for said he it is a shame to kill one of those large fat Cows merely for two mens suppers" So saying be leveled his rifle on the poorest and brot. her down. She was a heifer about 3 years old and but an inch of fat on the back. ...

_Only_ an inch, eh?

The frequent appearance of the word "fat" is interesting in itself. Type command-f (or control f on a Windows machine) and type "fat " (including a space after the word to take out compound words). I find the word used 38 times in the manuscript. It is always "fat" game that is sought -- and found.

\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*

The Natural History section has an item entitled "The Buffaloe or Bison" which is of interest:

> The flesh of the Buffaloe Cow is considered far superior to that of the domestic Beef and it is so much impregnated with salt that it requires but little seasoning when cooked. All the time, trouble and care bestowed by man upon improving the breed and food of meat cattle seems to be entirely thrown away when we compare those animals in their original state which are reared upon the food supplied them by Nature with the same species when domesticated and fed on cultivated grasses and grains and the fact seems to justify the opinion that Nature will not allow herself to be outdone by art for it is fairly proved to this enlightened age that the rude and untaught savage feasts on better beef and Mutton than the most learned and experienced Agriculturists …

> ... the rutting season of the Buffaloe is regular commencing about the 15th of July when the males and females are fat, and ends about the 15 of Aug. Consequently the females bring forth their young in the latter part of April and the first of May when the grass is most luxuriant and thereby enables the cow to afford the most nourishment for her calf and enables the young to quit the natural nourishment of its dam and feed upon the tender herbage sooner than it would at any other season of the year. Another proof is that when the rutting season commences the strongest healthiest and most vigorous Bulls drive the weaker ones from the cows hence the calves are from the best breed which is thereby kept upon a regular basis. In summer season they generally go to water and drink once in 24 hours but in the winter they seldom get water at all. The cows are fattest in Octr and the Bulls in July …

> … The grass on which the Buffaloe generally feeds is short, firm and of the most nutritious kind. The salts with which the mountain regions is much impregnated are imbibed in a great degree by the vegetation and as there is very little rain in Summer Autumn or winter the grass arrives at maturity and dries in the sun without being wet it is made like hay; in this state it remains throughout the winter and while the spring rains are divesting the old growth of its nutricious qualities they are in the meantime pushing forward the new - The Buffaloe are very particular in their choice of grass always preferring the short of the uplands to that of the luxuriant growth of the fertile alluvial bottoms. ...

\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*

The section entitled "The Crow Indians" is also perhaps enlightening.

The Crows were called "The handsome men" by the French and are generally described as having been tall, physically well-formed and symmetrical, and with well-proportioned facial features. They certainly sound healthy in Osborne's description:

> **Sickness is seldom found amongst them and they naturally live to a great age.** There is no possibility of ascertaining the precise age of any Mountain Indians but an inference may be drawn with tolerable correctness from their outward appearance and such indefinite information from their own faint recollections of dates as may be collected by an intimate acquaintance with their habits customs traditions and manner of living **I have never known a Mountain \[Indian\] to be troubled with the tooth ache or decayed teeth nither have I ever known a case of insanity except from known and direct causes.**

He goes on:

> I was upon one particular occasion invited to smoke in a circle comprising thirteen aged Crow warriors the youngest of whom appeared to have seen upwards of 100 winters and yet they were all in good health and fine spirits - they had long since left the battle ground and council room to young aspirants of 60 and under it is really diverting to hear those hoary headed veterans when they are collected together conversing upon the good old times of their forefathers and condemning the fashions of the present age ...

Well, maybe these old men weren't quite as old as Osborne thought, but these were evidently very fit old gentlemen and _compos mentis._ Other writers, such as Parkman (see _The Oregon Trail_) speak of aged but very active and healthy individuals among the Plain Indians. Parkman describes a woman in her 80s as having muscles like whipcord, as being the "moving spirit" of the camp, and as doing an incredible amount of demanding physical work, such as dismantling and erecting tepees. He also writes of a man of a similar age as having a large symmetrical physique, although somewhat emaciated by age, and as grabbing his weapons and running outside when a ruckus kicked up in camp.

I reckon some men in their forties in post-industrial society would probably be less active than it sound like this man was.

\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*

Never mind … what can old accounts of primitive life tell us? No wonder no-one bothers to read them any more. We're much better off, for we now have low fat diets and lipitor. All those symptoms you have are not owing to bad diet and a ridiculous lifestyle: it's just old age. We've never had it so good.

### Josh — 2014-02-04T23:11:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-of-a-trapper-by-osborne-russell.9603/post-108353>

Weston Price noted that the Inuit preferred the old male Caribou at the end of the summer because of the slab of fat on their backs. They also dipped everything in Seal oil. The really interesting stuff is the Oolichan fish oil commerce that took place on the West Coast for thousands of years....They brought marine fats throughout the Western US for thousands of years. Weston Price mentions tribes that would stop their wars temporarily to allow the inland tribes to come and gather the seafood needed to insure the health of their people. Seems indigenous people had worked a lot of things out.

### nonchalant — 2014-02-05T00:04:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-of-a-trapper-by-osborne-russell.9603/post-108362>

Thanks, Gagnrad. Interesting reading.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-05T08:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-of-a-trapper-by-osborne-russell.9603/post-108412>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=108353)
>
> Weston Price mentions tribes that would stop their wars temporarily to allow the inland tribes to come and gather the seafood needed to insure the health of their people. Seems indigenous people had worked a lot of things out.

Melanesians.

> My guide told me that it had always been essential, as it is today, for the people of the interior to obtain some food from the sea, and that even during the times of most bitter warfare between the inland or hill tribes and the coast tribes, those of the interior would bring down during the night choice plant foods from the mountain areas and place them in caches and return the following night and obtain the sea foods that had been placed in those depositories by the shore tribes. **The individuals who carried these foods were never molested, not even during active warfare.** He told me further that they require food from the sea at least every three months, even to this day.

[http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price7.html](http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price7.html)

Price continues:

> This was a matter of keen interest, and at the same time disappointment since one of the purposes of the expedition to the South Seas was to find, if possible, plants or fruits which together, without the use of animal products, were capable of providing all of the requirements of the body for growth and for maintenance of good health and a high state of physical efficiency

That ought to make one think.

### caroline — 2014-02-05T08:46:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-of-a-trapper-by-osborne-russell.9603/post-108419>

Mike this is so interesting ... thanks!

### Lahelada — 2014-02-05T11:22:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journal-of-a-trapper-by-osborne-russell.9603/post-108443>

Thank you . Very interesting. I will have to read the whole book now .

# Thread: Sugar
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar.9612/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-05T08:43:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar.9612/post-108418>

Andreas Eenfeldt has a nice quote from _JAMA_:

> We are in the midst of a paradigm shift in research on the health effects of sugar, one fueled by extremely high rates of added sugar overconsumption in the American public.
>
> Past concerns revolved around obesity and dental caries as the main health hazards. Overconsumption of added sugars has long been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD). However, under the old paradigm, it was assumed to be a marker for unhealthy diet or obesity. The new paradigm views sugar overconsumption as an independent risk factor in CVD as well as many other chronic diseases, including diabetes mellitus, liver cirrhosis, and dementia—all linked to metabolic perturbations involving dyslipidemia, hypertension, and insulin resistance. The new paradigm hypothesizes that sugar has adverse health effects above any purported role as “empty calories” promoting obesity. Too much sugar does not just make us fat; it can also make us sick.

[http://www.dietdoctor.com/new-study...\_campaign=new-study-sugar-cause-heart-disease](http://www.dietdoctor.com/new-study-sugar-cause-heart-disease?utm_source=rss&utm_medium=rss&utm_campaign=new-study-sugar-cause-heart-disease)

I fear it will still take a long time before the dinosaurs in public health admit that all excess carbohydrate is a potential danger. I'm sure the mainstream will still be urging me to eat grain or potatoes at every meal every day -- and plenty of fruit even in short light-cycles.

But I suppose some movement is better than nothing.

### sooperb — 2014-02-09T00:12:30+0200

I find the idea of sucking oil in and out of your teeth for 15 minutes until it foams quite unattractive. I take ubiquinol, largely so my OH will take it it. He's Type ll and takes ....... statins  I've tried to dissuade him and he's read most of the articles I have but his GP insists he needs to. Ubiquinol mitigates some of the effects.

It's supposed to help with gum health too but I think being proactive with dental care and diet is a much better idea than trying to put damage right. So says she who has a mouth full of fillings and several gaps where my teeth used to be.

# Thread: Fluoride neurotoxicity in children
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-neurotoxicity-in-children.9614/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-05T12:26:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluoride-neurotoxicity-in-children.9614/post-108444>

**Developmental Fluoride Neurotoxicity: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis**

> ... children in high-fluoride areas had significantly lower IQ scores than those who lived in low-fluoride areas. Subgroup and sensitivity analyses also indicated inverse associations, although the substantial heterogeneity did not appear to decrease.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3491930/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3491930/)

Got this off the Caveman Doctor's twitter stream. Glad he's onto this, too.

# Thread: Nutrients in food vital to location of early human settlements
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrients-in-food-vital-to-location-of-early-human-settlements.9615/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-05T13:33:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrients-in-food-vital-to-location-of-early-human-settlements.9615/post-108446>

This important work was published last December, but I missed it at the time.

> Research led by the University of Southampton has found that early humans were driven by a need for nutrient-rich food to select 'special places' in northern Europe as their main habitat. Evidence of their activity at these sites comes in the form of hundreds of stone tools, including handaxes.

[http://phys.org/news/2013-12-nutrients-food-vital-early-human.html#inlRlv](http://phys.org/news/2013-12-nutrients-food-vital-early-human.html#inlRlv)

So where were these sites?

> Professor Brown says: "**Our research suggests that floodplain zones closer to the mouth of a river provided the ideal place for hominin activity, rather than forested slopes, plateaus or estuaries.** The landscape in these locations tended to be richer in the nutrients critical for maintaining population health and maximising reproductive success."

To continue:

> Over 50 nutrients are needed to sustain human life. In particular, it would have been essential for early humans to find sources of protein, fats, carbohydrates, folic acid and vitamin C. The researchers suggest vitamins and protein may have come from sources such as **raw liver, eggs, fish and plants, including watercress** (which grows year round). **Fats in particular, may have come from bone marrow, beaver tails and highly nutritious eels**.
>
> The nutritional diversity of these sites allowed hominins to colonise the Atlantic fringe of north west Europe during warm periods of the Pleistocene. These sites permitted the repeated occupation of this marginal area from warmer climate zones further south ...

I think many people are still stuck with the image got off _The Flintstones_ of "cavemen" pursuing mammoths in a stone car ... Fred and Barney ... But quite often it seems that the real Paleolithic Diet turns out to look more like an Epi-Paleo diet.

The full paper is at PLOS One:

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0081476](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0081476)

### nonchalant — 2014-02-05T16:20:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrients-in-food-vital-to-location-of-early-human-settlements.9615/post-108468>

Except for the fact that folic acid does not exist in nature, I like it.

### yorkiegirl — 2014-02-05T19:31:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-hormone-help-please.9619/post-108503>

Hi everyone, been a while since posting but had some bloods recently done, and wanted some advice please.

Suffer from severe chronic insomnia, 9 years now, severe and extreme sleep deprivation over past 13 months, which has been truly horrific, days and days without, and then getting may 1 1/2 hrs sleep to 3 hrs only one night, then going days and days again without. The results of this are devastating, I have just aged myself 7-10 years by not sleeping, and who knows the damage on the inside. Made worse, i now realise by withdrawing from sedatives (that no longer worked), but getting anxiety and rebound insomnia on top of insomnia.

All my hormones will be out of balance due to not sleeping, especially low growth hormone,
the results I have no skeletal muscle or muscle mass, also oesteopenic.

I came off Trazadone last October, and the next day withdrawn, I had to literally eat 6 teaspoons of Salt.
My UK endo will not recognise any adrenal problems, I am hypothyroid. Have been unable to do my own 24hr salvia test due to all sedatives.

I have been doing what you all recommended, sun in morning/dark at night, cold showers (no facility for CT) blue blockers, bab etc. changed diet, been taking magnesium, vitamin d, back taking seriphos and zinc.
good sleep hygiene, tired but wired, no tolerance to any stress. Also take adrenal supplements in the morning.

Latest - Blood serum Cortisol - 358 (9am not fasting) no range given but endo says adequate
DHEA - 1.7 (1-7.0) - endo says ok normal - no reason to be taking (I am taking 25mg 7keto
have been for last 6 months, but obviously not making much difference -
am I safe up to the dose?

I just can't understand why he is not interested in the balance of the hormones, especially when I am unable to fall asleep without pills. No mention of whether high cortisol..I have so many symptoms.

My Oestradiol - 2 years ago - 69 (menopausal), but now 213 (says maybe still perimenopausal).
I know Oestrogen affects the thyroid, my thyroid levels are low Ft3 at 3.20 (3.50-6.50).

No Progesterone taken - but using Progesterone Cream - maybe I need Prenagalone?

Sorry, I am so exhausted, just don't know where/what to do next. Finding it so difficult to function.

I need some help, but know I'm not going to get it NHS way.

Also haven't really got a lot of money left to do a lot of private tests, but any suggestions to help my situation.

Thank you!

### sooperb — 2014-02-08T16:43:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-hormone-help-please.9619/post-109046>

Yorkie girl it sounds like you have got into a cycle that feeds off itself and you now have a situation that's difficult to crack. Have you tried buying a good mediation tape, Yoga Nidras work well, plugging yourself in when you go to bed or using a player that isn't too close to the bed and trying some relaxation techniques? If you're head is wired in to thinking you aren't going to sleep then chances are you won't, you need to break the cycle by concentrating on something completely different. You can sometimes get free meditation via apps or even youtube. Perhaps consult with a reflexologist who could work on the acupressure points for you or even try the acupressure mat that's mentioned in a thread by Caroline in the Cave.

You will apply an ice cold compress around your neck area around three hours before you go to your horizontal torture session, so that the torture begins early. You will think of nothing except repeat the above borscht recipe in your head , over and over.

Think that tonight you will have to read a book cover to cover and report the findings here tomorrow. You very likely have hormone imbalances galore but your most immediate problem is the anxiety which messes with your hormone balance. So go with the flow,you are NOT sleeping tonight, it is what it is , you have functioned on 2 hours sleep a night you will be able to do so again,it will get better just not tonight.

### nicld — 2014-02-12T16:52:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-hormone-help-please.9619/post-109792>

Easiest way is to do a blood test and see what the pregnenalone levels are. If below optimal, more then likely you have steal going on. I would figure out why and try and fix that but fortunatly, pregnenalone is not a negative feedback hormone, which means if you take it, it does not turn to something else of not needed.

As for the sleep, I agree with Lahelada, read something in low lights with blue blocker glasses on. I was the same way 7 months ago. It would take me FOREVER to fall asleep then only stay asleep for a couple of hours, wake up and maybe if I was lucky, fall back asleep a couple of hours later. Now, the sleep has greatly improved, still not the best but way better then before. It will take time but you can get it better.

Nicole

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-05T21:44:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108528>

I think it's possible to raise this interesting subject here. I suspect most of the Paleosphere would dismiss it out of hand.

I find myself bumping into references to these now and then. They often come up in the context of narratives about hunter-gatherers, for example. For example, it's said that the Bushmen women always know when the men have killed even though they might be miles away. I also remember reading of an someone who became interested in and went to visit the Bushmen and was told on arrival, "We've been waiting for you. I saw you before you came." I've also seen a description of an old Bushman throwing the bones for an Englishwoman.

> He asked her some intimate medical questions, and either guessed or through some sixth sense knew that she had had a major operation. He went on to specify the type of operation, correctly but rather embarrassingly since the seance was conducted in public around the camp fire. ...

Similarly, there's a well-known "Captivity Narrative" from colonial North America where a medicine man is described as heating and throwing some bones after two squaws reported seeing hostile Indians on the opposite bank of a river. The narrator says he looked a the bones and said the women were jumpy and it was nothing. The Indians had such confidence in the man that they all went to sleep without even setting a guard. In the morning, they found the tracks of two wolves.

One can always find reasons for disbelieving such stories, but sometimes they sound far more probable than otherwise. I also think that tribal sorcerers used deliberately to practice deception. If an Australian Aboriginal sorcerer produced a quartz crystal that he said he'd extracted from someone with a pain, then evidently he'd palmed it and was consciously practising deception. But I think there's often more than this going on.

These phenomena sometimes seem to happen closer to home, too. There's the "second sight" in the Hebrides off Scotland, for example:

[http://www.ambaile.org.uk/en/item/item\_narrative.jsp?item\_id=37792](http://www.ambaile.org.uk/en/item/item_narrative.jsp?item_id=37792)

One of the most interesting and persuasive accounts of an unusual and apparently inexplicable phenomenon I've ever read about, however, happened to a young woman from an Irish background in London. This was Dorothy Kerin, who said that she had been the recipient of a divine healing and afterwards went on to found a hospital, still in existence, where conventional medicine and prayer for healing were combined.

> In 1912 Dorothy Kerin was a 22-year-old who had been confined to bed for five years with tubercular meningitis, peritonitis and diabetes, when a miraculous cure restored her to full health overnight. Her healing earned her national attention and press headlines of ‘Miracle Girl’.

[http://www.burrswood.org.uk/about\_burrswood/our\_history](http://www.burrswood.org.uk/about_burrswood/our_history)

When it says "overnight", that's what is literally meant. Her doctor was called and thought the messenger had come for the Death Certificate. Arriving, he found Dorothy up and examined her, finding her apparently completely healthy:

> … he asked her to walk up a rather steep stairway, and when he saw her **run** up the staircase he could find no expression for his amazement, saying 'WHAT DOES IT ALL MEAN?' …

She was not only recovered but had, apparently, regained full muscle tone and become plump in 12 hours.

> Subsequent medical tests revealed no trace of the lesions and symptoms associated with her illness. It was a fully documented, miraculous cure and, as the news of Dorothy’s healing spread, she became an overnight celebrity, with newspapers camping outside her front door, and competing for interviews.

[http://www.burrswood.org.uk/news\_events/news/centenary\_of\_dorothy\_kerins\_healing](http://www.burrswood.org.uk/news_events/news/centenary_of_dorothy_kerins_healing)

See also:

[http://www.amazon.com/Dorothy-Kerin-Called-Christ-Heal/dp/0340006250/](http://www.amazon.com/Dorothy-Kerin-Called-Christ-Heal/dp/0340006250/)

Throughout this woman's extraordinary life, unusual events and odd coincidences seem to happen around her. She'd want to build an extension to the hospital and a cheque for the exact amount they needed would arrive, or unexpectedly some bricks would become available and there'd be the exact number needed to the last brick. And there was also a stream of healings.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-06T10:45:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108672>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=108545)
>
> Peter made a comment today in the follow thread that resonates with this one [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-new-old-fashioned.6069/page-14#post-108539](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-new-old-fashioned.6069/page-14#post-108539)

Thanks. That is remarkable. Is Peter perhaps in something of an alpha-theta state when he's running in the wild somewhere remote?

Talking of seeming to communicate (? if that's the right word) at some level with animals, I've seen one writer state that lions behave differently when the Bushmen are around. He said the lions would "misbehave" in certain ways, do things like foul waterholes, _except_ when the Bushmen were in the area. It was as if the lions knew they were there and were to a certain extent under authority. It's a totally unverifiable claim, of course. Interesting, though.

On the second sight: I did hear it doesn't happen so much now. I think the people in these remote parts of Scotland said decades ago that there were too many distractions around nowadays -- TV, things like that. Dr. Johnson wrote about the second sight in his _Journal of a Tour of The Hebrides_, and gave some cautious credence to it, which didn't do his reputation much good in such as sceptical age as the 18th century. I remember Johnson said that the people there seemed very much to live in the present moment.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-06T13:51:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108693>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=108678)
>
> Mike - have you read the book ...The cosmic Serpent by Jeremy Narby
>
> This is so totally fascinating to me.... I had never thought of any of this until Jack did a webinar ...and then had Erwan Lecorre on the Live Q&A with him. It just blew me away.....

No, I don't know that book.

Erwan is great, isn't he? Those videos of natural movement are something else.

> I have talked about this in my journal .... I went to an evening with a Psychic - about 50 people were there. I have never, ever experienced anything like that....talk about cosmic energy and quantum entanglement!

That's interesting. I used to work with someone who was a psychic. She did tell me things about myself that she couldn't really have known.

But here you start to hit on different view about these things. AFAIK, the "second sight" comes unsought. Offering readings seems, possibly, to be taking a different kind of approach. It's discouraged in the Judaeo-Christian and Buddhist traditions (although Buddhism does, in practice, get mixed up with shamanistic practices in siome parts of the world) -- and I think Mircea Eliade says is the Yoga Sutras of Patanjali, too -- but is quite in order in Altaic religion, of course.

There's an ode of Horace's -- it's the famous _carpe diem_ ode -- where there seems to be a suggestion that some things are supposed to be hidden from us. There's a beautiful translation of the ode by C. J. Herington -- at the bottom of this page:

[http://www.newcriterion.com/articles.cfm/C-J--Herington--an-homage-3330](http://www.newcriterion.com/articles.cfm/C-J--Herington--an-homage-3330)

I wonder if Lynne was someone Herington knew?

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-06T16:33:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108702>

If you take a 30,000 foot view of humans on earth you find that it's only in the last few hundred years that most of us living in the Western world really separated ourselves from nature and animals.
Some traditions within this region still maintain a closer connection, but it is the shaping of our thoughts by various cultural, religious, political and economic structures that changed our thinking about the paranormal, communication between species and non-local connections etc.
Those people whose world view falls outside this construct haven't the same issues and if you think about it, it is really just the quantum world writ large. As we loose our connection to nature we loose the range of sensory abilities and physical capacity that most of us in the way past would have taken for granted. Now it is fragmented knowledge; esoteric and exotic.....

### sjoshua — 2014-02-06T19:11:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108720>

When I am ocean swimming, sticking keto, and lying in the grass under the summer sun with only shorts on, I feel "closer" to my surroundings, if that makes sense... I hear the bees, the squirrels become friends, and the breezes feel alive. Sometimes just being in that state puts my mind at ease, and allows me to drift to a reality where time is irrelevant. (<- I think this is something humans truly lost when we started to live disconnected from nature... to me it feels like the start of the perfect night's sleep or the meditative rhythms our brains are meant to experience)

I agree PrAna303 is onto something, with the visibility aspect. But to put ourselves in the animals shoes would be more helpful to really understand. Humans have developed excellent visual ability with our complex eyes, whereas much of the animal kingdom relies on its other senses. I'd imagine a human who is out of balance would smell/sound/look/eminate that fact, and stick out to the point that nature knows they don't belong there. In the same way, a human in balance would flow with the environment. This isn't necessarily saying the animals wouldn't still be weary, but they'd be weary in a different way more akin to predator/prey vs being confronted with the chaos of the unknown.

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-06T20:51:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108731>

I think each of us has this innate ability to connect on different levels. the problem is that we just don't perceive that it can actually happen. I think deja vous is a remnant of this capability we've forgotten to nurture. We've been taught from an early age in modern society that this doesn't exist. so in our world today it doesn't exist for most. But stop and think about Schrodingers' Cat experiment. is it alive or dead in the box? it depends upon your perception. so society doesn't buy in or think this 2nd sight or senses beyond the "5" exist. Yet there are those who are good at perceiving what's really going on, they can divine the information. Some are more sensitive due to how they were raised (religious beliefs vs encouragement to discover beyond what religion holds - same goes for science as well)

I saw this great video on FB -but can't find the actual link to post here - of a man "Dancing" with a horse. it was a beautiful their communication was intuitive. they knew each others next step before they executed. Obviously training went into their effort - but you could see the communication. it was like they were connected by nothing, yet they knew everything the other would do.

Seeing the past or the future is one thing, seeing the entanglement btwn the two and how they lead to one another is critical to understanding where we're at today, where we'll be tomorrow, and where we'll be in the future.

Grandmother spider weaves the web upon which we walk. The question is how does our action upon the web influence what she weaves, as well as where we walk?

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-06T23:37:04+0200

(don't know if that was clear) Dancing with horses requires you to be totally grounded and totally open to all possibilities at the same time.....you can't be behind or ahead of what is, but there is always movement even when you are still..and you have to maintain mindful awareness while operating from your heart....no wonder it's the best thing ever! I like Grandmother spider's web walk...more quantum wisdom.

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-07T00:37:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108773>

That's one thing I do miss from childhood. is getting up and riding on a regular basis. I loved it. It was awesome to be able to get up on a horse and give them 100% trust. especially when show jumping. I road this one horse a big warmblood named Sailor he was a bay with a white blaze and stalkings. He could jump anything. One day we were working in the ring and my trainer told me to start running the course. So I did. I'd only been jumping seriously with Sailor for about 6mths and had graduated up to 5Ft obstacles. well the previous rider had been riding Grand National event hight obstacles and they hadn't been reset. we cleared all the obstacles no rails down. all because I knew he could jump anything and I trusted him 100%. and in giving that trust he trusted me. my trainer was floored. From then on, until I stopped riding, we jumped event hight obstacles.

its amazing what you can do when you don't have any limitations or restrictions...

I do love Grandmother Spider. She came and settled in and waited with me, spinning her web with each pregnancy. She still comes when change is on the horizon. We catch and release all spiders b/c we don't want to hurt the Grandmother.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-07T08:44:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108846>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=108731)
>
> I think each of us has this innate ability to connect on different levels. the problem is that we just don't perceive that it can actually happen.

I think intellectuals tend not to, because it doesn't fit with the dominant ways of seeing in those circles over the past 300 years or so. And you could probably make someone like Richard Dawkins apoplectic by even suggesting such things are possible.

I'm not so sure abut the average person. Certainly a fair number of bereaved people describe seeing dead spouses. Whether they do or not … I think this kind of question remains where William James left it, that's to say, I've no way of disproving someone else's experience but neither can it be intellectually coercive for me. And some experiences may be illusory, some real, but as for knowing which is which.

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b00s9ftw](http://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b00s9ftw)

John Tanner's captivity narrative is interesting. In Chapter XI he writes:

> Two years previous to this time, a man of our band called Ais-kaw-ba-wis, a quiet and rather insignificant person, and a poor hunter, lost his wife by death, and his children began, even more than formerly, to suffer of hunger. The death of his wife was attended with peculiar circumstances, and Ais-kaw-ba-wis became melancholy and despondent, which we attributed to the sluggishness of his disposition, but he at length called the chiefs together, and with much solemnity announced to them that he had been favoured by a new revelation from the Great Spirit. ...

[http://imp.lss.wisc.edu/~jrvalent/AIS/Assets/Pdfs/BookTanner.pdf](http://imp.lss.wisc.edu/~jrvalent/AIS/Assets/Pdfs/BookTanner.pdf)

Tanner's scepticism seems entirely justified. And yet it's not that Tanner is sceptical of Ais-kaw-ba-wis because he thinks that there's nothing, as it were, beyond the end of his nose. Turn to Chapter XV and we have:

> My sister Lucy had, the night before my arrival, dreamed that she saw me coming through the corn field that surrounded her house.

And, of course, there is a massive amount of personal testimony for that kind of thing.

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-07T15:15:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108874>

my personal experience, and that of different family members indicate the ability is there.

when I lived on Okinawa - there was a day we were transferring "hot" cargo over to the ammo storage area - and on that trip I saw an entire Japanese Garrison marching for war. they walked right through our truck. Having seen ghosts before I knew what they were. by my NCO had never seen them before and freaked out. and Kept asking "did you just see that? did you feel that? OMG OMG" all the time crossing himself. Okinawa is full of ghosts - enough so they actually have ghost tours for tourists - they take you to battles and other places and you pretty much see ghosts. There are even times when the Okinawans call out the dead, and celebrate them and feed them in certain ceremonies.

I've had other experiences. I was living with my grandfather- helping him get back on his feet after my grandmother died. IT was her birthday - and I wasn't able to sleep so I got up and went into the living room to sit. as I walked into the living room I realized the kitchen lights were on. I had turn them off when I went to bed. as I investigated - the oven was on, and in it cookies were baking. there were also fresh cookies on the counter. as I reached for one - to verify that I wasn't dreaming my grandmothers ghost came upon me and smacked my hand and told me to leave the damn cookies alone. needless to say I was quite stunned. I went back and sat in my grandmothers chair. about 40 mins later my grandfather got up in the middle of the night, noticed the light - came in and looked at me, then looked in the kitchen and said the following " I seem MaryLou's been making cookies tonight"

The next morning I asked him about that - and he said she did that every night before her birthday, when she was alive.

I've had conversation with other cousins who've been at the house since - and they've had similar experiences with my grandmother, until my grandfather died.

This is one side of the family. on my mom's side I have a cousin with 2nd sight. she can tell you who's going to win football games and by how much. She knows if things are ready for pick up or if they're not. Whats interesting, is that when people ask her for her predictions she looses her ability. but if its just general conversation say about something she's not interested in, she can give you the score. I've seen her tell my grandmother the total number of wins the cleaveland indians would have in a single season. its crazy.

So is it the places that make us more sensitive? part of me say yes - there are certain places that its easier for both sides to mingle - the veil is lifted.
Are some more sensitive? I think so. I think for some it flows through families.

but I do think all can cultivate this if they choose. The challenge is many don't know how to access this component of their brain. and of course many don't want to for then they'll actually have to see what's before them instead of the reality their mind created.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-07T15:59:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108878>

That's a really interesting post.

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=108874)
>
> when I lived on Okinawa - there was a day we were transferring "hot" cargo over to the ammo storage area - and on that trip I saw an entire Japanese Garrison marching for war. they walked right through our truck. Having seen ghosts before I knew what they were. by my NCO had never seen them before and freaked out. and Kept asking "did you just see that? did you feel that? OMG OMG" all the time crossing himself.

That's particularly telling, since one could well believe that someone seeing a departed husband or wife was a hallucination (and perhaps wish-fullfilment). And maybe it sometimes is. But I don't suppose you were expecting to see the Imperial Japanese Army coming marching through.

> I've had other experiences. I was living with my grandfather- helping him get back on his feet after my grandmother died. IT was her birthday - and I wasn't able to sleep so I got up and went into the living room to sit. as I walked into the living room I realized the kitchen lights were on. I had turn them off when I went to bed. as I investigated - the oven was on, and in it cookies were baking. there were also fresh cookies on the counter. as I reached for one - to verify that I wasn't dreaming my grandmothers ghost came upon me and smacked my hand and told me to leave the damn cookies alone. needless to say I was quite stunned. I went back and sat in my grandmothers chair. about 40 mins later my grandfather got up in the middle of the night, noticed the light - came in and looked at me, then looked in the kitchen and said the following " I seem MaryLou's been making cookies tonight"

That one is very strange.

> This is one side of the family. on my mom's side I have a cousin with 2nd sight. she can tell you who's going to win football games and by how much. She knows if things are ready for pick up or if they're not. Whats interesting, is that when people ask her for her predictions she looses her ability.

That doesn't surprise me. It seems that perhaps you can't always "use" these things.

I read a couple of books on that American man -- can't remember the name: I think it was French in origin -- from the South who would go into a trance and give healing remedies. (The WAPF doctor mentions him here and there.) \[EDIT: just remembered Edgar Cayce\] What was quite interesting was that the healings sound like they did work. Yet he also gave a string of prophecies that seem to be absolute balderdash. He also seems to have come into contact with a man who give him some kind of theosophical explanatory framework and gone off into all that, which one can take ... or more probably leave. But the actual readings, and never mind explanations, do seem interesting.

> So is it the places that make us more sensitive? part of me say yes - there are certain places that its easier for both sides to mingle - the veil is lifted.

Isn't there a George Eliot short story called _The Lifted Veil_ ?

> Are some more sensitive? I think so. I think for some it flows through families.

This is what I'd understood. My father seems to be a little bit so and one of my sisters, too.

> but I do think all can cultivate this if they choose. The challenge is many don't know how to access this component of their brain. and of course many don't want to for then they'll actually have to see what's before them instead of the reality their mind created.

I expect so. I'm not sure how much one should, at least in some respects, if one could ... We already opened that side of the topic with the comment I made earlier about the different views of some of these kinds of phenomena is different traditions, and you do in a way above with the comment on the football games. Some experienced meditators mention stuff like this cropping up and say you should ignore it and carry on ...

Do you know Dunne's book _An Experiment with Time_ ? He was an Irish areonautical engineer who was interested in dreams and thought there were fragments, sometimes quite small ones, of future events in them.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-07T16:52:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108889>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=108881)
>
> I've not heard of Dunne's book. I'll add that to my reading list...  it seems my list keeps getting longer here....

It was quite influential in its day. A lot of writers were influenced by it. C. S. Lewis was one; other names escape me at the moment.

Dunne also said you can get a kind of prevision with books. I seemed, unexpectedly, to get that the other day listening to an audiobook. It was one of the _Gormenghast_ novels. The narrator said that the old Earl was going to give the child a birthday gift, and I thought, "He's going to give him a pinecone." It just came into my mind. And that was what he did give him. Maybe it was just a coincidence.

### nonchalant — 2014-02-07T16:53:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108890>

A bit related to this is my mother's family friend, Perry Philbin. He healed by touch, and saved my uncle's life after their doctor gave up on him. My aunt was uncomfortable sitting next to him at the dinner table, she said her whole side (near him) would go numb. Perry got a lot of suspicion from others, so he never took payments for helping people. My grandparents gave his family food during the depression, and they remained lifelong friends.

### Josh — 2014-02-07T22:15:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-108942>

If you are interested in learning techniques that allow one to reliably experience these things, I would recommend studying with Dr. Shannon, he is an ex-marine sergeant and interrogator. It is quite real and practical. I have seen him take a diverrse group of 15 people including me into these things in 4 x 5 day trainings. He is a rare man.

[http://www.medicalqigong.org/aboutus.htm](http://www.medicalqigong.org/aboutus.htm)

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-08T12:01:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-109028>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=108942)
>
> ... he is an ... interrogator.

That's not surprising perhaps. You'd certainly have to attend to people very closely if you were interrogating them.

That extraordinary Frenchman who could, apparently, teach people languages in days did that after WWII:

[http://www.michelthomas.com/](http://www.michelthomas.com/)

It was said you couldn't tell Michel Thomas a lie; he would always know. Not that there's anything necessarily paranormal about that: it could be a matter of noticing small cues that would pass most of us by. There's some interesting stuff on that in one of those books Daniel Goleman wrote with the Dalia Lama -- maybe this one:

[http://www.amazon.com/Destructive-Emotions-How-Overcome-Them/dp/0747561826/](http://www.amazon.com/Destructive-Emotions-How-Overcome-Them/dp/0747561826/)

It seems experienced meditators can spot expressions that flicker very briefly across people's faces.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-08T12:22:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-109032>

Here's another interesting story. Does anyone know this one?

> In 1959 a young monk named Tsung Tsai (Ancestor Wisdom) escapes the Red Army troops that destroy his monastery, and flees alone three thousand miles across a China swept by chaos and famine. Knowing his fellow monks are dead, himself starving and hunted, he is sustained by his mission: to carry on the teachings of his Buddhist meditation master, who was too old to leave with his disciple.
>
> Nearly forty years later Tsung Tsai — now an old master himself — persuades his American neighbor, maverick poet George Crane, to travel with him back to his birthplace at the edge of the Gobi Desert.
>
> They are unlikely companions. Crane seeks freedom, adventure, sensation. Tsung Tsai is determined to find his master's grave and plant the seeds of a spiritual renewal in China. As their search culminates in a torturous climb to a remote mountain cave, it becomes clear that this seemingly quixotic quest may cost both men's lives.
>
> **Amazon blurb**

[http://www.amazon.com/Bones-Master-Journey-Secret-Mongolia/dp/0553379089/](http://www.amazon.com/Bones-Master-Journey-Secret-Mongolia/dp/0553379089/)

I think I've read that book three times now. It's wholly remarkable. Crane as told by himself I don't like, and he really does himself no favours with the sequel to this book where he abandons his wife and daughter and embarks on a series of drunken wanderings. But Crane's a writer and no doubt. He tells the Ch'an monk's story extraordinarily well. And it's gripping on many different levels. First, it's that as a story of escape and perseverance in the face of hardship -- Tsung Tsai had to flee the Great Leap Forward in China or face state-sponsored murder. And his journey to Hong Kong and escape involved such hardship that it's surprising he survived that. Secondly, you do seem to get some insight into Tsung Tsai's state of mind. Again, Crane deserves some credit for pulling that off, specially as one sometimes wonders how much of this he himself grasps. (Crane describes himself as a Zen Buddhist but says he doesn't meditate. It seems a little self-consciously precious for a Westerner to label himself as a _particular type_ of Buddhist -- particularly when you reflect that Ch'an (or Zen) _means_ meditation! I'm a "meditation Buddhist" who doesn't meditate! ... Sometimes it seems he has that conception that some Westerners have that "Zen" is about smart repartee. But I don't think that's what _koans_ are about at all.)

Finally, there's plenty in the book that doesn't fit into a post-Enlightenment view of what the world's like at all. I'm not sure how truthful George Crane would be as a writer, but he relates an extraordinary episode where they travel to Hong Kong and Tsung Tsai apparently has some kind of spiritual tussle with a former pupil who's "gone bad".

### Josh — 2014-02-08T17:15:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-109052>

I can assure anyone interested that studying Medical Qi Gong is nothing like being or learning interrogation, except perhaps of one's own mind/body....

> it becomes clear that this seemingly quixotic quest may cost both men's lives

all good quests, and life on Earth cost us our lives, it is worth remembering....George Gurdjieff said the constant remembrance of this is the only hope of coming out of mankind's current situation....

### Josh — 2014-02-08T17:26:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-109058>

Consider that Buddha and Mahavira and many of the great "enlightened" masters came from the warrior class. The intense crystallization that comes from rigorous discipline and training seems to prepare a being to crack open freeing their mind and heart. A diamond or crystal will crack with one blow of a hammer, mud and softer things will not. Heraclitus said one needs to dry one's soul like a bundle of sticks so that it will catch fire with a single spark.

### Josh — 2014-02-08T18:30:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-109067>

And things are only "paranormal" if they are not "normal" for a given individual.....

### athene noctua — 2014-02-09T00:51:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-109147>

josh, your recommendations are right on, this leads on a path i've found i've begun to follow.

the crystallization of consciousness really resonates with me - it's like removing entropy or blockages. i first discovered this through intense rock climbing, but it expanded other areas of my life. took away my fears to let me achieve new and crazy things. now i've begun to tap into my sensory awareness (have been classified as a "highly sensitive person" perception-wise. is both bad and good) and use it to paradoxically relax, give up worry and let my connection to the universe surprise me with countless synchronicities!

would love to train my perception further, though. somehow this fits perfectly with the discoveries jack is giving us. i think meditation and higher states of awareness, for example, are no less a part of CT or magnetic fields and resonances...

thanks to all for creating this space for thought.

### sooperb — 2014-02-09T20:52:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-109307>

I have been told by different people that I have the ability to be a good healer. I'd love to have such a gift, I have no idea how you develop it though or even if it's true. I've been to circles where everyone else is talking about their experiences, I've not been able to contribute, it just doesn't happen for me no matter how much I'd like it to yet apparently I have the gift? Maybe there is a right time and mine hasn't arrived. I've got plenty of dead relatives but they never appear lol.

### Josh — 2014-02-09T21:35:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranormal-phenomena.9620/post-109313>

If you want a comprehensive understanding, take a look at Jerry Alan Johnson's books on Medical Qi Gong. "The Magus of Strovolos" is a treasure....

# Thread: Flax seeds and sunflower seeds... Bad?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Daydreamer89 — 2014-02-06T01:49:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108616>

I've been consuming a lot of flax and sunflower seeds within the past year. Need some help regarding the safety of these foods. I've read conflicting stuff on the Interweb. Thank you.

### caroline — 2014-02-06T02:04:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108619>

what do you mean by a lot? How do you feel when you eat them? What is your intuition telling you?
What are your health concerns?
Please start a journal in the journal section .... Dr. Kruse needs context.......

### Daydreamer89 — 2014-02-06T02:10:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108622>

Well, I've been on a ketogenic diet for the past year. I generally stay within 30g to 100g of carbs a day. Mentally, I've never felt better. On flax seeds I've been doing pretty well, no adverse effects in mental performance. With Sunflower seeds I feel slight sinus pressure. I buy both seeds raw and usually prepare them cooked (ground fresh and made into gluten free "bread" and waffles) except for sunflower seeds that I eat raw from time to time (sprinkled in salads). I will usually eat flax and sunflower 1-3 times a week. I have yet to get any bloodwork done but I am looking to do sometime soon.

### caroline — 2014-02-06T02:16:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108623>

are you eating epi paleo with a seafood template? Do you have Dr.K.'s book? it will give you a great overview of all of this. Have you listened to Jack's podcasts? lots of quick and easy info - go to the home page..... and all his blogs....

Welcome!

It sounds as if the seeds aren't a problem ..... Dr. K. talked a little bit about them in the January 2014 webinar.....

### Daydreamer89 — 2014-02-06T02:35:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108626>

Yes I am currently reading the book, have gone through some of the blog posts and have listened to all of Dr. Kruse's podcasts. My concern was regarding the PUFA content in flax and sunflower seeds and was hoping for some advice wether it is okay to eat them. If not, I will drop these foods from my diet.

I'm doing everything as I should. I eat mainly grass-fed beef as my protein source but I do eat fish, usually tilapia, sardines or salmon at least once a week. I supplement with fish oil as well. Lots and lots of coconut oil and grass fed butter. I also get my fats from almonds, macadamias, brazil nuts, pistachios, pecans, hazelnuts and peanuts. My issue has always been my acne and since I've done keto it has gotten a lot better . Pops up less frequently but it's still an issue at 24 years old. I've done everything to try and solve this issue but with limited success which leads me to think that it is largely hormonal. I have some progesterone cream on the way that I just ordered and have been supplementing with Maca to help with balancing hormones.

I've been doing CT for almost two weeks now and I feel amazing and my body looks more toned even though I'm not exercising as heavy. My one issue with CT is dry, cracking skin that've developed on the side of my cheeks and my hands. Been using Neem oil to combat this problem and it seems to be helping. Hopefully this subsides as I become more cold adapted.

### caroline — 2014-02-06T02:42:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108627>

Can you eat seafood everyday? suboptimal seafood is way better than GF beef.
Should you be eating peanuts?
What is your non native EMF exposure? at home? at work?
How much nuts/seeds do you eat?
Have you had hormone testing done? How is your cortisol? DHEA? melatonin? inflammation?
Are you observing lite cycles? no artificial lite after sundown?
do you have blue blocking glasses?
do you drink R/O water?

Food is only 10% - you need to examine other things in your life.....

take it slow .... keep reading and asking questions!

### Daydreamer89 — 2014-02-06T03:11:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108632>

Eat seafood everyday? I can sure try.

Peanuts? Well, I've never had a problem with them although according to David Asprey of Bulletproof Exec, they have mycotoxins and aren't very optimal. May consider cutting them.

I am currently a college student. Exposure to computers is a constant for me. I am looking to purchase a red lightbulb and buy some blue-blocking glasses to combat this problem. At sundown I will turn out the lights and keep a dim lamp on unless I have school work to do.

I predominantly drink R/O water. Bottled when I'm out if forget to pack some.

Seeds/Nuts comprise a majority of my diet. I usually take in 2,000 calories and 600-1,000 of that is nuts/seeds. The rest is veges, meat or fish, cage free eggs, low sugar fruit (infrequently) and lots of GF butter and coconut oil.

I have yet to get any testings done but looking to do so sometime soon.

I am 5'2, muscular build, at 140 lbs. looking to cut weight. Last time I check I was at 26 percent BF.

### caroline — 2014-02-06T03:39:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108640>

You are female?

I would cut out peanuts and re engineer your diet...... this is what you want -
high fat, medium protein and low carb

I would reverse the whole nut/veggie thing

this is how your day should look...

get up and go outside and look at the sun
eat a BAB within 30 minutes of rising
oxy
leave at least 4 hours between meals and before bed - absolutely no snacking between meals
no artificial lights after sundown

do you have your computer wired in?
you need to address non native EMF as best as you can - really important

maybe try to use nuts and seeds as a bit of garnish? concentrate on high fat and seafood.

You want to be able to enter the PPP ... be in ketosis

sounds like you have a bit of detox from C/T

you are doing great! what are you studying?

### Daydreamer89 — 2014-02-06T03:51:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108643>

I am unclear on what BAB and Oxy is, could you please explain?

Internet connection is wifi. :/ not much I can do about this in my present situation.

Currently in a Nursing program.

Thank you so much! Greatly appreciate it.

### caroline — 2014-02-06T08:11:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108658>

Okay ....first things first .... I am pretty sure Dr. Kruse would say that nursing is not a good idea. Shift work and working around a crap load of EMF in a hospital. Huge recipe for disaster.

Can you get your laptop wired in? there is a "how to" on the home page

BAB = big ass breakfast .... have you read the leptin RX? 50g of protein within 30 minutes of rising. leave at least 4 hours between meals and before going to bed and NO snacking.

oxy = oxytocin = orgasm .... you need to stimulate the pharmacy in your brain! twice a day...

what else can I help you with?

my pleasure...

### Inger — 2014-02-06T10:25:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108669>

I would not recommend to eat sunflowerseeds... at least not much, and cooked/old

Flax is also not good in huge amounts I am sure. Goes rancid very fast if you buy ground flax. So if you use them take care they are fresh (!) and ground right before use, do NOT bake them but use them raw instead. Maybe raw and whole they would be best IMHO. And not too much. I do not really eat seeds at all. Seldom I make halva from raw organic sesame seeds with raw honey, it is yummy. But that is more for family. Anyways, if you want to eat seeds, be very careful with quality or they are going to hurt you. Buy as fresh as it gets, and never ground. Do not cook. The same with nuts.

If you have too much O6 you should not have seeds at all for sure...

If I were you I would just leave them out for a while and see what happens

PS. Nipple stimulation one minute every morning is a great way to get oxytocin too!
Ask Jack.

### Daydreamer89 — 2014-02-06T14:53:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108694>

Got it! I'm definitely lowering my consumption of all seeds and if I do consume I will do so raw. Sucks, I bought a 4 pack of sunflower seeds and now I can't eat them. Well, I could always set up a birdhouse in my backyard and feed 'em to the birds, that'll put them to good use.

### JanSz — 2014-02-06T16:56:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flax-seeds-and-sunflower-seeds-bad.9624/post-108704>

Daydreamer89, 24yo, Joined: Yesterday
\-------------
I am 5'2, muscular build, at 140 lbs. looking to cut weight. Last time I check I was at 26 percent BF.
\--------------
Are you man or woman. If man, aim at 10-15% body fat, woman little more bf
\--------------
Stop supplementing with fish or krill oils.
There is a hype within medical community and oil industry is making $$ on this misinformation.
We need all, including fatty acids, but we need just right amounts.
Fish or krill oils contain EPA & DHA fatty acids, problem is that there are mega-doses of EPA (in relation to what we may need),
that
suppress (good) Omega6 fatty acid, AA, Arachidonic Acid).

Eat lots of sea food, do not eat supplemental oils, very careful with seeds and nuts.
Seeds and nuts are ok, but cannot be a major part of ones diet.
Do not eat (significant amounts) of oils made of seeds or nuts.

preferably, when in doubt
do (good) fatty acids analysis.

Good fatty acid analysis are getting harder to come by.
Recently I seen laboratory in Belgium excluding the DHA fatty acid from analysis.

That is similar to a seminary that is supposed to educate priests but not allowed to talk about God.
Or Hell without Satan.
\--------------
Why are you using progesterone cream? Post your blood tests.

acne
newer ever use accutane, Proscar, Propecia, Avodart, finasteride, duosteride, any hair remedies containing above

above are 5aR inhibitors, that also includes (bio & human-idendical) progesterone.

....
